> From The only really "available" spec, which isn't the original, this is how you embed multiple CTCP messages inside one.

Following that link, it appears to be a pretty decent spec, and consistent with the implementation as found in this library. Given that it is the actively published version, I would give it deference over other specs that have been deleted and are only available in the way-back machine.

As a result, I've added a link to that spec to the ctcp module, for clarity.

Looking at that spec, and in particular at Example 3 in Quoting Examples, there is specifically an example of a client signaling a USERINFO request alongside text of a message, which seems contrary to the assertion that

> Because there are actually multiple different types of these CTCPs, ...you should not blindly respond to any possible CTCP purely based on the presence of delimiters.

and

> The real reason for allowing embedded CTCP inside messages is for text formatting.

Therefore, I remain unconvinced that the existing implementation is faulty.

> you are strongly discouraged from responding to any CTCP message sent to a channel automatically on practically every server in existence.

I'm not sure what you're recommending here instead. I don't believe the IRC client library has specific behaviors for responding to any message, and it's the responsibility of the individual implementations to respond to the relevant CTCP events as appropriate, and only for servers to which that client is connected. The assertion is unfounded. If you believe there's something that should be done to better handle CTCP messages, please suggest what the problem is and how you propose correcting the problem.

That's not a risk at all, I'm just reporting what people said to me, I think they didn't use the appropriate words for that.

About the handling itself it's the first time I see a message being parsed as a CTCP request because it contains SOH chars in the middle by a maintained client/library, however it's my own judgment and not related to the specs.

I completely understand your point of view, and if this is following the specifications then there's no problem at all! :ok_hand:

You can find me on Freenode if you'd like to have a direct conversation about that, I don't know if it still belongs to a GitHub issue now.

commit b4b97d0dd43192848407bce4aa3eb8f6779b456f adds code to try to manually install eigency in setup.py using pip, reporting an error if it fails: 

```python
# the setup file relies on eigency to import its include paths for the
# extension modules. however eigency isn't known as a dependency until after
# setup is parsed; so we need to check for and install eigency before setup.
import importlib
try:
    importlib.import_module('eigency')
except ImportError:
    try:
        import pip
        pip.main(['install', 'eigency'])
    except ImportError:
        raise ImportError('The eigency library must be installed before FEW. '
                          'Automatic install with pip failed.')
finally:
    globals()['eigency'] = importlib.import_module('eigency')
```

I'm thinking particularly about a piece of code like the below (taken from the django-pdb project - https://github.com/tomchristie/django-pdb)

```python
        p = pdb.Pdb()
        p.reset()
        p.set_break(filename, lineno, temporary, cond, funcname)
        sys.settrace(p.trace_dispatch)
```

This code can be extended to use `IPython.core.debugger.Pdb` instead of `pdb.Pdb`, which behaves mostly like ipdb, but it lacks some of ipdb's features such as colours. It would be useful to have (for instance) an `ipdb.Pdb` object, which extends the IPython object but adds all of ipdb's nice features.

As things are currently, if I wanted to extend this to behave like ipdb, I would basically end up having to copy-paste a large amount of the ipdb code, just to duplicate the same functionality.

Sure, sorry about being unclear. My impression is that the original fix to help with nose test output was to change the stdout pointer:

```python
def update_stdout():
            # setup stdout to ensure output is available with nose
            io.stdout = sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
```

When that was found to cause problems in some cases (specifically with ipython notebooks), it was changed to this:

```python
if 'nose' in sys.modules.keys():
        def update_stdout():
            # setup stdout to ensure output is available with nose
            io.stdout = sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
else
        def update_stdout():
            pass
```
When I say the current implementation, I mean that perhaps another fix that doesn't require contextual stdout shifting might work better.

Let me give an example for curio tasks/futures.
They are seem to be effectively the await/spawn for the sync world.

```python
async def someasync():
   await sleep(5)
   return 1

task1 = kernel.run(someasync(), timeout=0)  # Non-blocking
task2 = kernel.run(someasync(), timeout=0)  # Non-blocking
kernel.run() # Blocking
res1 = task1.result
res2 = task2.result
```

_just call task2.join()._ 
you  mean don't use gather at all? i prefer using gather because for me it better conveys that all tasks are running freely as opposed to dabeaz's code in https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/36 where result = await task.join() gives the 'impression' that the tasks are getting serialized. 

i guess the 'join' hard wires my brain into thinking blocking without recognizing the await next to it. maybe i should just get used to it. but then again the next colleague will stumble on it again... ;)

I've hit an issue with limit of 200. Its not exactly obvious.

On device connection pygatt does characteristics discovery. It sends `characteristics` to gatttool and device very quickly spews out ~1300 bytes (11 handles). In handling characteristics output pygatt is able to capture:
```
DEBUG:pygatt.device:Looking up handle for characteristic af230003-879d-6186-1f49-deca0e85d9c1
DEBUG:pygatt.backends.gatttool.gatttool:Found characteristic 00002a01-0000-1000-8000-00805f9b34fb, value handle: 0x5
DEBUG:pygatt.backends.gatttool.gatttool:Found characteristic af230004-879d-6186-1f49-deca0e85d9c1, value handle: 0x1d
```
That is the first and last handle only the other 9 are simply lost. Increasing this parameter to 2000 results in:
```
DEBUG:pygatt.device:Looking up handle for characteristic af230003-879d-6186-1f49-deca0e85d9c1
DEBUG:pygatt.backends.gatttool.gatttool:Found characteristic 00002a00-0000-1000-8000-00805f9b34fb, value handle: 0x3

```
That solves the immediate problem.

But don't think this is correct solution, a device can have many many more characteristics. Furthermore in event subscription a device each event is ~100 bytes per event (with the gatttool pretty formatting), which means that mere 2 event packets will wipe our search buffer. If we are to choose a reasonable default I'd propose something 3-4 order of magnitude bigger. But ideal solution would avoid hard coding buffer size maybe we can manage by deleting read messages or expiring them by time rather than buffer size.

No worries! I didn't want to overwhelm with too many things at once. The documentation is build from the Jupyter notebooks which are in the docs/sources/user_guides subdirectory. If there's an API change, the `make_api.py` scripts needs to be run first as it generates the markdown files of the docstrings that get then included in the jupyter notebooks (at the very bottom). Then, after the Jupyter notebook is run, the script `ipynb2markdown.py` is run as `ipynb2markdown.py --ipynb path_to_the_updated_notebook` to generate the updated markdown file, which gets later converted to HTML/the web documentation when a new mlxtend version is released.

I am seeing a 20% increase in the time it takes to do a pyserial write. I am using 3.4.2. In PyMata callback mode, the increase is barely noticeable by eye (I am turning a potentiometer to adjust the brightness of an LED attached to a PWM pin).

However, in polled mode where I continually perform a pyserial write in a tight loop, the delays are seconds in length, and this is unusable. Running the same code in Windows with 3.4.2, there is no noticeable delay 

Running the same tight loop code in python 2.7 on Linux , there are no noticeable delays. From my profiling, the amount of time 3.4.2 is spending in a linux select call during writes is very large compared to 2.7.8.

So my conclusion is, if you need to run PyMata on 3.4 use callback mode. If you need it in a polled type environment, try it out. Depending upon the application it may or may not work well.

It appears that the delays in Python 3.4 are application dependent. It took me a while to find the link that discusses the delays, so I am assuming that for most applications, these delays are not noticed. I am hoping that someone on the Python team acknowledges the problems in Linux and fixes them in a later release.

If I can figure out where to report the problem to the Python team I will issue a trouble report.

I think you can just add a def("__init__", another_constructor_that_takes_int32) line to the rectangle to add an overload. This way you don't need to introduce more types.  Although a better thing to do is probably to add a new construction function that takes 4 boost::python::object instances and appropriately converts them to ints.  

You can certainly submit some pull requests that make whatever you think are improvements to the python API and I'll review them.  Making the rectangle's constructor take more int types sounds like a good idea.  I'm not so sure about the to_array() API though.  Allowing someone to reference the elements of a rectangle via [0] or [1] instead of .left() or .top() does not seem like a usability improvement.  What use cases does this support?  If there are some compelling use cases then sure, but just because OpenCV does it doesn't mean it's a good idea on its own.  A lot of the API designs in OpenCV are horrible.

Those errors indicate that something has gone horribly wrong with your compiler install.  They aren't related to dlib.  From the sound of the errors, I wouldn't be surprised if your copy of GCC is incapable of compiling any C++ programs, even "hello world".  

I would recommend reinstalling GCC.  

I should also point out that a fresh install of Ubuntu 16.04 comes with g++ installed and that copy of g++ works.  So it's very likely that something you installed subsequent to installing a fresh copy of Ubuntu corrupted your system.  I would try to figure out what that is and avoid installing it again.

1. uninstall boost-python for python2   
`brew uninstall boost-python`   
2. install boost-python for python3   
`brew install boost-python --with-python3`
3. change name of `libboost_python3` to `libboost_python`   
`cd /usr/local/opt/boost-python/lib/`
`ls`, and got something like this:   
```
libboost_python-mt.a            libboost_python3-mt.a
libboost_python-mt.dylib        libboost_python3-mt.dylib
libboost_python.a               libboost_python3.a
libboost_python.dylib           libboost_python3.dylib
```
backup boost-python for python2 and change boost-python for python3:   
```
mv libboost_python-mt.a libboost_python2-mt.a & cp libboost_python3-mt.a libboost_python-mt.a
mv libboost_python-mt.dylib libboost_python2-mt.dylib & cp libboost_python3-mt.dylib libboost_python-mt.dylib
mv libboost_python.a libboost_python2.a & cp libboost_python3.a libboost_python.a
mv libboost_python.dylib libboost_python2.dylib & cp libboost_python3-mt.dylib libboost_python.dylib
```   
4. clone dlib from github and cd dlib   
`python3 setup.py install --yes USE_AVX_INSTRUCTIONS`
   
Done!

@tdryer, do you have an objection to this patch by itself?

It might seem a bit intrusive, but the good thing about it is that it won't change a given user more than twice (no name → numeric, numeric → real name, with a one-way path). So if the same user later reappears with no name for some reason, they won't change back.

I think it's better for applications to handle creating the cache directory rather than the hangups library. If hangups does it, then there's no way to opt-out of this behavior and passing the wrong path could create unexpected directories rather than an error.

There are two ways to make it so that you don't need to use sudo (and this is just a linux thing, nothing to do with rshell):
1 - Add your user to the dialout group. If you do `ls -l /dev/ttyUSB0` you'll typically see something like this:
```
crw-rw---- 1 root dialout 188, 0 Dec 18 20:54 /dev/ttyUSB0
```
This says that the user root and the group dialout have read/write access to /dev/ttyUSB0. Note, when adding yourself to a group, you'll need to logout completely and log back in for it to take effect. You can use the `id` command to verify what groups your user is a member of.

2 - You can add a file to /etc/udev/rules.d which will change the permissions of the /dev/ttyUSB0 file. Follow the directions here: https://github.com/micropython/micropython/wiki/Board-STM32F407-Discovery#programming-from-linux-via-dfu (note that you don't need to install dfu-utils)

> I am also thinking of monkey-patching the touch() function. This is possible, right?

I think if you take the JSONStorage as it currently is and remove all the stuff that is used to write new entries, you won't need to monkey-patch the touch() function. You could go with something like this:

```python

class JSONStorageReadOnly(Storage):
    """
    Store the data in a JSON file.
    """

    def __init__(self, path):
        """
        Create a new instance.
        Also creates the storage file, if it doesn't exist.
        :param path: Where to store the JSON data.
        :type path: str
        """

        super(JSONStorage, self).__init__()
        self._handle = open(path, 'r')

    def close(self):
        self._handle.close()

    def read(self):
        return json.load(self._handle)

    def write(self, data):
        raise RuntimeError('JSONStorageReadOnly cannot write data')
```

@davidmankin you can get SID by `import os; os.getsid(pid)`.

Session ID is a PID(PGID) of the session leader.  The concept was introduced by SVR4, and BSD adopted it, too.  So it's not Linux specific.  Session manages Process Groups, Process Group manages Processes.  Normally the shell is the session leader that interacts with the controlling terminal.  In case of daemon process, it gets its own session ID so it's detached from the invoking session.

I changed my mind once again: unexpectedly returning a different type (`str` or `unicode`) depending on the string we're dealing with is not a consistent API and may lead to unpleasant user code like `if isinstance(s, 'unicode'): ...`. Also, that would affect Windows users only, which is even worse.
I decided that on Python 2 we will always return a string (`str` type) encoded by using `sys.getfilesystemencoding()` codec and I will make this clear in the doc.
It must be noted that this is the behavior we're already using on Linux / Python 2:
- https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/8006cfb8beeaff436ec80193e0f57fb46c2bb0c7/psutil/_pslinux.py#L59
- https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/8006cfb8beeaff436ec80193e0f57fb46c2bb0c7/psutil/_pslinux.py#L752

...and other platforms as well (since we're not returning unicode anywhere) so what I'm I'm trying to do here is to be consistent with the established behavior in a cross-platform fashion.
It must be noted that this issue does not exist on Python 3 since Python 3 fixed unicode handling once and for all.
The relevant PR which I've just merged is: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/676

Prosody trunk now has full support for Private/Public persistent PEP. I built support for specifying ``access_model`` in ``aioxmpp.PEPClient.publish``. We should discuss the API for PEP-based storage.

I’m thinking of subclassing the claim PEP node descriptor with two subclasses:

* MultiItemPEPStorage which is used for things like Bookmarks 2 and provides an interface to list items in the node, delete items, add items etc.
* SingleItemPEPStorage which is used for most other PEP based stores where all data is crammed in a single item. It forces the ID to be "current" according to XEP-0060 singleton use and offers fetch, put and an on_changed event.

A using service would then do something like this:

```python
class BookmarksService(...):
    xep0084_node = MultiItemPEPStorage(node="storage:bookmarks", private=True)
    xep0402_node = MultiItemPEPStorage(node="urn:xmpp:bookmarks:0", private=True)

    async def fetch_items(self):
        return join_the_items(old=(await self.xep0084_node.fetch()), new=(await self.xep0402_node.fetch()))
```

hello,
you don't have to use the message loop. it's just there for your convenience. getting the same message again when manually calling get updates is not a bug. you need to give the get updates method an offset to skip messages that you have seen. you can read more about it here : https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#getupdates

> In order to avoid getting duplicate updates, recalculate offset after each server response.

for your second question, the handle is exactly that function that you have in your code. it doesn't have to be called handle, you can call it whatever you want. telepot calls that when a new message is sent to your bot to "handle" the message.

as for how it works you can check out the source:
https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/master/telepot/__init__.py#L491

it may seem a little complicated because it has many options and works for both webhook and normal mode, but it basically calls get updates in a loop and calls your handle function every time a new message is received.

All of this looks normal to me. By default the consumer consumes from the latest messages. It also does all broker api calls inside `poll`, so what you see happening here is (1) producer sends a message, (2) broker increments highwater offset, (3) consumer asks for latest offset and gets the highwater, (4) consumer requests messages starting at highwater offset, which does not include the first message.

So your solutions here are either to consumer.poll() before sending your first message, or configure your consumer to consume from the earliest available offset, not the latest, via `auto_offset_reset=earliest`.

Hi @mohierf , thanks for the (very interesting) feature proposal.

Let me clarify your needs:

1) You want to uses Glances as a NSCA client. Glances will send periodically (using the -t <sec> refresh command line tag) stats to a NSCA server. To do this, Glances should be modified as following:
- Create an new export mode to NSCA server. Glances will be run as a agent using the command line:

    glances -t 300 --quiet --export-nsca

with the new following configuration in the glances.conf file:

    [nsca]
    # Configuration file for the --export-influxdb option
    # https://www.nsca-ng.org/
    host=nscaserver
    port=5668
    passwork=mysecret

There are existing Python NSCA client (to be tested, it is a C binding): 
- https://github.com/weiss/nsca-ng/tree/master/python
- https://pypi.python.org/pypi/send_nsca

2) For this need, i think that you have all you need in the current Glances version.
- first off all, create a central NSCA server (https://www.nsca-ng.org/)
- on each node, install nsca_send (https://www.nsca-ng.org/documentation/send_nsca.html)
- on each node, install Glances
- define action thought the configuration file (example for memory critical). Not tested...:
   
    [mem]
    critical=90
    critical_action=echo -e "host\tstatus\tCritical memory: {{percent}}" | send_nsca -H <nscaserver> -p <port> 

- run Glances (without any GUI):

    glances --quiet

I only ask because some of the line numbers in that stack trace look a little odd...

But regardless, my initial thought would be that you aren't setting `trakt.api_key`, or you're setting it to a Trakt APIv1 API key. If you haven't already done so, I'd try running `trakt.init` and running through the manual steps that it provides you for authorizing PyTrakt

```python
import trakt
from trakt.tv import TVShow
my_api_key = trakt.init('myusername')

it_crowd = TVShow('The IT Crowd')
tvpeople = it_crowd.people
```

The `trakt.init` function will put your API key in place when you run it, but you should only need to run that once. After that, it's up to you to decide how you want to hang on to that key, but going forward you'll just need to do 
```python
import trakt
from trakt.tv import TVShow

trakt.api_key = my_api_key
it_crowd = TVShow('The IT Crowd')
tvpeople = it_crowd.people
```

Ah, interesting. I wasn't aware of that feature. PowerPoint is still the source of surprises after all these years :)

There's no API support for this yet.

If you wanted to develop a workaround function for it, the first step would be to discover how it's implemented in the XML. You could do a diff between a before an after presentation using `opc-diag`, or you could just inspect the XML in suspected areas. My first thought would be that there's something in the `presentation.xml` part that identifies hidden slides by slide-id. The other possibility is that there is something like a `<p:hide val=true/>` element toward the top of the `slide{N}.xml` part. If I didn't find it there I'd look in the `app.xml` or other settings parts.

`opc-diag` is good for that kind of casual inspection. I usually use the command:

```bash
opc browse my-presentation.pptx presentation.xml | vim -
```

Which feeds it right into my editor with code folding. Your mileage may vary depending on your editor's capability.

The `diff` command on `opc-diag` neccesarily picks up a fair number of "noise" diffs. To reduce those, create a minimal presentation (one empty slide) and save it. Then hide the slide and save it as the "after" version. This should reduce the noise enough to pick out the operative change in the XML.

`opc-diag` is here: https://github.com/python-openxml/opc-diag

There's a link to the docs on the GitHub home page and you can install it from PyPI using `pip`.

In fact, you should not be using `self.error` directly here. After having looked again at *pyfolio* today, the reports seem to focus on *daily* and larger timeframes for the analysis. And I say *seem* because I haven't looked into every possibility and it might be that one could get reports with intraday statistics.

*pyfolio* does internally *resample* anything you pass to it. It is, today, visible even without looking at the source code because there is a deprecation warning about the resampling method used inside the python notebook.  And because of this resampling it shouldn't matter it you pass intraday data to it, because it will be upsampled to at least daily.

In the test I ran earlier today, the modifications I made kept, for example, the *transactions* with the full *datetime* information, i.e: a 30 minute timestamp. The report generated later by *pyfolio* had *daily returns* and *annual returns*.

I did test using

  - `cerebro.addanalyzer(bt.analyzers.PyFolio, _name='pyfolio', timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Days)`

and

  - cerebro.addanalyzer(bt.analyzers.PyFolio, _name='pyfolio', timframe=bt.TimeFrame.Years)`.

But the *daily* nature of the *pyfolio* report and the *resampling* suggest that the best approach would be:

  - `timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Days`

  - Keep the timestamps as they are originally seen in the data stream and rely on the *resampling* performed by pyfolio itself.

Hi guys, sorry for jumping in. Possibly you guys talk about slightly different issues?

Definately agreed that it is foremost a question of strategy design to think well into which data feed to buy and at what time. 

Mementum, you were very careful in your design to prevent any look ahead bias with using negative indexing (indeed an advantage over many other platforms). 
Now if I understand the point: in reality it is not possible to buy at the OPEN of a bar which has already started to 'happen' at the point in time when the buy signal is perceived (any buy signal to occur, say at 9:36h from the more granular data feed -1 min bars-, will in reality have no change to buy into the less granular time frame - 10 min bars - for the 10-min OPENING price which occured at or brefore 9:30h, at 9:30:01h it might be outdated already by the arrival of a new price which we do not know. At 9:36h the opening price is possibly history

In fact, to take it further, it would never even have a chance to buy into the opening price of any contemporaneous bar of any feed of any granularity, may they be related or not. This is since you can not even be sure that the opening price is indeed prevalent at 9:30h sharp. If at 9:30h no trading took place, the opening price reflects the price of the last trade. It could have already happened at 9:29h.
So, even when the decision to buy is taken at exactly 9:30h as in the first snippet above:
`        if self.data0.datetime.time() == datetime.time(9, 30) and not self.bought:
            self.buy()`
you cannot be sure to get a fill at the opening price.

So if I got the point correctly, I would agree that under some circumstances this could introduce a look ahead bias.

Your desing is well layed out overall, so along these lines to stay coherent, would allowing to trade only at the close of a concurrent bar (and possibly additionally test for bar volume>0 to be sure a very old last price is not just carried through time with no actual trading) remedy the issue?

Here is a minimal example : 
```
from pynput import mouse

def on_move(x, y):
    print('Pointer moved to {0}'.format(
        (x, y)))

def on_click(x, y, button, pressed):
    print('{0} at {1}'.format(
        'Pressed' if pressed else 'Released',
        (x, y)))
    if not pressed:
        # Stop listener
        return False

def on_scroll(x, y, dx, dy):
    print('Scrolled {0} at {1}'.format(
        'down' if dy < 0 else 'up',
        (x, y)))

from pynput import keyboard

def on_press(key):
    try:
        print('alphanumeric key {0} pressed'.format(
            key.char))
    except AttributeError:
        print('special key {0} pressed'.format(
            key))

def on_release(key):
    print('{0} released'.format(
        key))
    if key == keyboard.Key.esc:
        # Stop listener
        return False


import kivy
kivy.require('1.10.0')

from kivy.app import App
from kivy.uix.label import Label


class MyApp(App):

    def build(self):
        # Collect events until released
        with mouse.Listener(
                on_move=on_move,
                on_click=on_click,
                on_scroll=on_scroll) as listener:
            listener.join()

        # Collect events until released
        with keyboard.Listener(
                on_press=on_press,
                on_release=on_release) as listener:
            listener.join()
        return Label(text='Hello world')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    MyApp().run()
```

@netjunki I am still trying to figure this one out myself (as someone not expert on working with certs/SSL configs, it's a bit slow).  I do know that there are some subtle differences in curl vs. libcurl in pycurl: I hit issues with handling of request size and continuation with Django, where it worked in curl but not the PyCurl and I had to explicitly set an option to override it. 

General rule of thumb is that at least one pycurl option exists to override a specific handling. 

If it's any help, the included Django test app offers a testing environment you can use to validate behavior with at least *one* webserver, and it runs from the functionaltest.py tests. 

I do see that in the libcurl docs there are many reasons for that particular error:

> **CURLE_SSL_CONNECT_ERROR (35)**

> A problem occurred somewhere in the SSL/TLS handshake. You really want the error buffer and read the message there as it pinpoints the problem slightly more. Could be certificates (file formats, paths, permissions), passwords, and others.

One useful troubleshooting tip:  this will print out the fill HTTP traffic back and forth (everything!) and has been very useful for me in debugging HTTP configuration issues!

```python
pycurl_connect.setopt(pycurl.VERBOSE,True)
```

After inspecting the pull request #133, I think we should make the `--make-whitelist` feature more general by splitting it into two parts:

* `--make-whitelist` writes a whitelist to stdout in the following format: `myfunc  # unused function (foo.py:42)`
* `--coverage-xml` lets Vulture ignore functions that are marked as used in the coverage XML report

I think both features are useful on their own and when used in conjunction. Separating them also simplifies the documentation and implementation, I think.

I like this idea. What do you think of the idea of allowing developers to set the JSON library via a parameter?
For example, something like:

```python
chatbot = ChatBot(
    'My Bot',
    # ...
    json_library={
        # json.loads and json.dumps have different method names in various JSON libraries
        'loads': 'import.path.for.json_library.loads',
        'dumps': 'import.path.for.json_library.dumps',
    }
)
```

The advantage would be that it reduces the amount of code that has to be added to ChatterBot to support different JSON libraries in the future. It also gives the developer more control.

Sorry for the delay, I finally got a chance to look at this properly. 

It looks like the issue is with your origin header. What is the 'Origin' you are sending the CORS request from? Your configuration will only allow browsers to issue a CORS request from "http://127.0.0.1:8080" to your flask-cors app (running by default on localhost:5000). 

Example:
If a browser were currently looking at an html page on "http://127.0.0.1:8080", and the JS on that page issued an XHR to e.g. localhost:5050/token, they would send a request like this, and receive a similarly succesful response.
```
➜  flask-cors-test curl --include -X GET http://127.0.0.1:5000/test --header Origin:http://127.0.0.1:8080 --header 'Authorization: Bearer 12345678'
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 20
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://127.0.0.1:8080
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
Server: Werkzeug/0.12.1 Python/2.7.13
Date: Sat, 22 Apr 2017 20:47:26 GMT

{"test": "testing"}
```

But, if instead your browser is pointing to something even slightly different, e.g. localhost:8080 (which would resolve to the same thing on your machine), the browser will see the issue you are reporting. The browser will issue a command similar to this, and receive a non-cors response:
```
➜  flask-cors-test curl --include -X GET http://127.0.0.1:5000/test --header Origin:http://localhost:8080 --header 'Authorization: Bearer 12345678'
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 20
Server: Werkzeug/0.12.1 Python/2.7.13
Date: Sat, 22 Apr 2017 20:49:33 GMT

{"test": "testing"}
```

### Suggestion: `panflute.fenced_yaml_filter`?

I don't think there's a natural way to change it without breaking backward compatibility. Do you think it's ok to provide an alternative version of the yaml filter, say, `panflute.fenced_yaml_filter`, that *requires* both an opening row of `---` and a closing row of either `---`/`...`, that can be at arbitrary location (much like what pandoc is currently doing). i.e.

	~~~csv
	---
	source: somefile.csv
	title: Some Title
	...
	index,data
	1,2
	~~~

or

	~~~csv
	index,data
	1,2
	---
	source: somefile.csv
	title: Some Title
	...
	~~~

or

	~~~csv
	index,data
	1,2
	~~~

Are all perfectly fine. For this,

	~~~csv
	index,data
	---
	source: somefile.csv
	title: Some Title
	...
	1,2
	~~~

I'm not sure. Probably we can allow this to happen (basically extracting all YAMLs and the remaining becomes the data.

In the case of

	~~~csv
	index,data
	---
	source: somefile.csv
	...
	1,2
	---
	title: Some Title
	...
	~~~

Even more troublesome, but in the spirit of the current pandoc behavior (that YAML can appears anywhere), this should also be allowed.

### Other questions

1. Currently the YAML filter returns a string, even if the format is, say, `0.25,0.25,0.25,0.25`. It seems that it is not really parsing the YAML (otherwise it should be a list)? And even better, it might try to convert to float whenever possible. Currently, I use `.split` and `float()` to do the conversion. (and for alignment I need to use `.strip` to remove white spaces.) I think it would be better if the data are properly parsed.

2. In your example above, you used `source: somefile.csv`. Did you have another version of panflute CSV filter? I don't seem to find it from your examples in this repo or panflute-filters. By the way, I intended to write my csv filters to be backward compatible to yours (and may even be compatible with pandoc-placetable and pandoc-csv2table, depending on the needs).

Thx @eggachecat for your tips! Exactly right, not all programs will get into the tournaments. And if they do, they will likely be modified by one of the mutation or cross-over mechanisms, and their children's fitness isn't guaranteed to be better. `est._programs` contains all of the generations in a list of lists structure. These aren't public attributes but you can tool around there as much as you like.

Another point is that bootstrapping your samples might change these results generation to generation even if the result is the same! If you OOB sample size fluctuates a lot then your solution may be overfit. Might be worth upping the parsimony if that is the case. Or get more data :-)

Another way to increase the likelihood of a superior program getting through evolution unchanged is to reduce the probabilities of some of the cross-over or mutation actions. The remaining probability goes to "reproduction" which means simply cloning the winner of a tournament into the next generation.

Since the tf.rgba matrix is shape (256,4), we're restricted to 1D transfer
functions (TF: R ->R^4). Multidimensional transfer functions (TF:R^n->R^4)
take inthere's account not just data value, but other quantities such as
data position (x,y,z) or gradient magnitude/direction, hessian norm, etc.
It seems that these are better suited to edge detection between distinct
homogeneous materials, as is the case in the human skull or tooth, for
example.

One way to implement this would be to turn tf.rgba into shape (256,256,4),
and have the second column be gradient magnitude. A more general way would
be to provide an interface for directly setting rgba values at each voxel.
(I'm not sure if this is already possible - probably from JS, but not
Python?) Then, we could create a function which takes data values as an
input, calculates whichever derivatives are of interest, and then sets the
voxels directly as a function of the data values and derivatives.

Hi David,

Unfortunately there isn't any export functionality available in Python-Redmine right now, but there is already a feature request for that (see issue #58).

But you can easily implement it yourself, Python-Redmine gives you all the available data, so you just have to implement a csv writer to save this data into a file, I can give you a quick example how this could look like:

```python
import csv
import redmine

rm = redmine.Redmine('https://your-redmine-server', username='user', password='pass')

project = rm.project.get('project_identifier')

with open('/absolute/path/to/your/file/{0}.csv'.format(project.identifier), 'wb') as csvfile:
    writer = csv.writer(csvfile)

    for issue in project.issues:
        writer.writerow([
            issue.id,
            issue.subject.encode('utf-8'),
            issue.description.encode('utf-8'),
            issue.status.id
            # other issue attributes if needed, use
            # dir(issue) to get all available attrs
        ])
```

* add hooks in backend for renderer to use to decode values

Hello,

Thanks for the detailed report.

> The issues appears during hot plug events, when a device is plug-in while listening, trying to get the device.getSerialNumber() raise USBErrorBusy.

`getSerialNumber` is special in that it opens the device and issues a synchronous "setup" request to the device to get the string descriptor containing the serial number. And synchronous calls are just asynchronous calls plus blocking event handling called internally.

Grepping a bit in libusb-1.0 code, I stumbled on [this](https://github.com/libusb/libusb/blob/b99391deb4894bbfff59fab985c99fc55a43169a/libusb/sync.c#L89). This is not visible in the [on-line documentation](http://libusb.sourceforge.net/api-1.0/group__syncio.html#gadb11f7a761bd12fc77a07f4568d56f38), so I guess it was not re-generated from source for a while (it dates from a commit from late 2015). @dickens : could you refresh the docs ?

This would likely explain why creating a separate context helps: I believe each context uses/needs its own event handling, so another context will not "see" the original one is still doing event handling.

This means that another fix could be to append the device received during hotplug event and only use it outside of event handling call stack (move even handling to a thread and use some IPC to notify the even happened available, or process the list when `handleEvents` returns...).

@drallensmith the loop used by `_worker()` is a `while self.working` loop. The condition (`self.working == True`) is checked after one genome has been evaluated or `self.inqueue.get()` could not return a genome to evaluate within `0.2` seconds. This means that the worker loop checks at least once every `0.2` seconds whether it should still be working and return otherwise. You could actually simply stop the worker by setting `self.working` to `False`.

"Process" would be the most accurate term, but it still has two issues:
1. The auto-mode determination can only check whether the node should be the master node, it can not check if a process should be the master process. This may lead to confusion.
2. A slave node may start multiple worker processes to evaluate the genomes.
However, both of these issues are minor and a simple note or hint in the documentation should be enough to avoid confusion.
Another alternative would be to add these terms as *(Computer)Node* in the glossary and add something like *Not to be confused with computernodes, which are used by...* to the normal node definition in the glossary.
But changing the term should be enough.

Regarding `multiprocessing.cpu_count()`: Good catch. But i think you misunderstood something about the return value of `multiprocessing.cpu_count()`. It does not return the number of physical cores, but the number of cores as the OS sees them. For example, when using processors with hyper-threading, each core can execute two threads. The OS thinks it has twice the number of cores it actually has. I have an I7-4770k (4-core) with hyper-threading, which means i have 4 cores which can each execute two threads. The OS think i have 8 cores.
This is probably also the case for your processor. If you have a 2 core machine with hyper-threading (most Intel processors use hyper-threading), your OS will think it has 4 cores.

Of the Django apps that I've taken a look at, Bootstrap 3 seems to be the most commonly supported framework. That doesn't mean that older versions of those apps aren't being run, just that most apps have updated their templates to assume Bootstrap 3.

I would not be surprised to hear about internal tools using no framework, Bootstrap 2, Foundations, or something else, but I think the thing to keep in mind is that this template isn't very complicated and - being a Django<sup>1</sup> template - is easily overridable. That basically means that we are really only discussing what to support *by default*.

For this PR, I wanted something that looks decently pretty "out of the box" with a minimum of fuss but still worked without dependencies, and Bootstrap 3 filled that role perfectly.

So to be perfectly clear: I don't see it being worth it to switch to something else.

I did the worst fix ever but hopefully it can spark a better implementation. 

I've created a flask config called SWAGGER_BASEPATH which basically is concatenated to generate the swagger.json URL.

So in my case:
```
app = Flask(__name__)
app.config['SWAGGER_BASEPATH'] = '/contacts'
api = Api(app, version='1.0', title='TodoMVC API',
    description='A simple Contacts API',
)
```

And then everything works like a charm without needing to use ReverseProxied, CORS or anything else.

My small implementations (happy to adapt or if the thing already existed please point me to it):


in flask_restplus/api.py:
```
@property
    def specs_url(self):
        '''
        The Swagger specifications absolute url (ie. `swagger.json`)

        :rtype: str
        '''
        url = url_for(self.endpoint('specs'), _external=True)
        if self.app.config.get('SWAGGER_BASEPATH', ''):
            prefix = url.split('/swagger.json')[0]
            url = prefix + self.app.config.get('SWAGGER_BASEPATH', '') + '/swagger.json'
        return url
```

in flask_restplus/swagger.py:

```
basepath = current_app.config.get('SWAGGER_BASEPATH', '')
        if not basepath:
            basepath = self.api.base_path
            if len(basepath) > 1 and basepath.endswith('/'):
                basepath = basepath[:-1]
```

And that's it.

@swathimithran, thanks for the example.  Your help is sincerely appreciated!  As a quick fix, I made it so it won't capture ordinal numbers that end in `th`.  You can view the change here: https://github.com/DanielJDufour/date-extractor/commit/68deab4d342cec33e690e19cb0562e9ae0b52b44

However, we will need more discussion on what rule should be used for what precedes the 2 digits.  Here's a few examples of 2 digit years:
- 12/23/09
- 15-11-21
- 9/1/99 22:00 
- paper_170120 (in a filename)
- taxes_16.docx

Basically, I'm afraid that if we make the rule too strict, people won't be able to parse dates out of filenames.

Here's a few possible solutions.  What would you like?

### Option 1
Add a parameter `source_type`, which could be `filename`, `filepath`, `text`, `html`, or `javascript`.  This way you could customize it, so you can restrict the rules to certain types of sources.  Here's an example of what this could look like
```
from date_extractor import extract_date
string = "I went to my first basketball game in 1990.  My favorite player had number 34."
date = extract_date(string, source_type="text")

string = "my_resume_091216.pdf"
date = extract_date(string, source_type="filename")
```

### Option 2
The second option could be allowing users to override existing patterns.
```
import date_extractor
date_extractor.patterns['y'] = "\d{4}"
string = "The author asserts that the earliest encounter never happened (43)"
date_extractor.extract_dates(string)
```

### Option 3
The third option could be returning a confidence level, `low`, `medium`, or `high`.  You could then filter depending on your need.
```
from date_extractor import extract_dates
string = "He was selected by the Sacramento Kings in the 2nd round (48th overall) of the 2004 NBA_Draft. A 6'4' guard from Morehead State University, Minard was signed by the Kings in July 2004, but they waived him in November the same year, and so far he has never appeared in an NBA game."
dates = extract_dates(string, return_confidence)
# dates = [(datetime.datetime(1948, 1, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=), 'low'), (datetime.datetime(2004, 1, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=), 'medium'), (datetime.datetime(2004, 7, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=), 'high')]
```

### Option 4
I'm open to suggestions as long as it doesn't prevent users from extracting years out of filenames.

Which do you prefer? What do you think?

When SQLAlchemy executes 
```python
result = cached_connections[connection].\
    execute(statement, params)
```
from `sqlalchemy/orm/persistence.py:834`
it is calling 
```python
conn.execution_options(
            compiled_cache=base_mapper._compiled_cache
        )
```
from `sqlalchemy/orm/persistence.py:1117`
which in turn is calling `Connection._clone()` from `sqlalchemy/engine/base.py:135`.

This results in a Connection object being passed to Continuum's `track_association_operations`  to be missing from `self.units_of_work` at `sqlalchemy_continuum/manager.py:376`, but the contents are the same, since Connection's __dict__ was copied.

I propose either switch from using Connection object to track UnitsOfWork to `conn.connection` (`ConnectionFairy`) or `conn.engine`, or make a hook in Continuum's `make_versioned` like such:
```python
    def track_cloned_connections(c, opt):
        for connection, uow in versioning_manager.units_of_work.items():
            if connection.connection is c.connection:  # ConnectionFairy is the same - this is our clone
                versioning_manager.units_of_work[c] = uow

    sa.event.listen(
        sa.engine.Engine,
        'set_connection_execution_options',
        track_cloned_connections
    )
```

I think it would be quite complex to support that.

When we use a `zcml:condition`, there is often a module, which is only imported when the condition is true.
This module can therefore import from a package which is not in the normal requirements list, for example the package used in a `installed PACKAGE` condition. This is perfectly fine.

But it means that for supporting zcml conditions consequently, z3c.dependencychecker would need to know if a module is only imported when a condition is fulfilled, which makes it very complex.

**Example:**
The above example (plone.schema / plone.supermodel) shows this perfectly:
```xml
    <configure zcml:condition="installed plone.supermodel">
      <utility
          component=".handler.URIHandler"
          name="plone.schema.URI"
          />
    </configure>
```

This ZCML will only import `handler.py` when `plone.supermodel` is installed.
The first line of `handler.py` is:
```python
from plone.supermodel.exportimport import BaseHandler
```

When we only fix the ZCML parsing, the python parsing will still tell us that we require `plone.supermodel`  because of the import in `handler.py`.
In order to handle this we would have to know that `handler.py` will only be imported when `plone.supermodel` is available because of the ZCML definition.

This gets a little too complex, at least with the current regex based implementation. We would have to build an AST or something, where we can track the dependencies between code and ZCML etc. :confused:

I don't think this is something that can done, and I suggest considering following a more restful pattern, specifically, using HTTP methods to capture "operations" rather than the URI.

Something like:
`url(r'^tubes/(?P<operation>(create|delete|get))`

Should be

GET /tubes (list)
DELETE /tubes/<id>
POST /tubes (create)
GET /tubes/<id> (individual object)

As for the second example, something more "restful" would mean using a query parameter or filter for the entities, or, making the routes based on the resource.

i.e. `/session?entity=cluster`
or /cluster/session

I have a workaround for this issue which I'll detail below. I'm new to these libraries, so I'm having trouble figuring where the proper fix location would be. If anyone has an idea, let me know, I'm more than willing to fork, fix, test, and PR.

My current fix:
Inject a step into python-social-auth:
```
SOCIAL_AUTH_PIPELINE = (
    'social.pipeline.social_auth.social_details',
    'social.pipeline.social_auth.social_uid',
    'social.pipeline.social_auth.auth_allowed',
    'social.pipeline.social_auth.social_user',
    'social.pipeline.user.get_username',
    'social.pipeline.user.create_user',
    'social.pipeline.social_auth.associate_user',
    'path.to.your.view.save_oauth',
    'social.pipeline.social_auth.load_extra_data',
    'social.pipeline.user.user_details',
)
```
As this step occurs after associate_user, we have the newly created user model as well as the token available to us in the request.
```
@partial
def save_oauth(strategy, details, user=False, *args, **kwargs):
    request = kwargs.get('request', None)
    if user and request:
        access_token = request.get('token', None)
        social = user.social_auth.get(provider='github')
        social.extra_data['access_token'] = access_token
        social.save()
        return { 'is_new': False, 'user': user }
    return { 'is_new': True, 'user': None }
```

Here a toy example where I first write the data to disk, then load it back (assuming this would be a different session...) and iterate over it with `NDArrayIter`

```python
import numpy as np
import h5py
import mxnet as mx

N = 1000

X = np.random.normal(0,1,(N,12))
y = np.random.randint(0,2,N)

# write data to file
with h5py.File('myfile.hdf5', "w") as ofile:
    ofile.create_dataset("X", data=X)
    ofile.create_dataset("y", data=y)

# load data from file
ifile = h5py.File('myfile.hdf5', 'r')

X_h5 = ifile["X"]
y_h5 = ifile["y"]

batch_size = 200
dataiter = mx.io.NDArrayIter(X_h5, y_h5, batch_size=batch_size)

for iBatch, batch in enumerate(dataiter):
    print(iBatch, batch.data[0].asnumpy().shape)

ifile.close()
```

Commit #52a2ff3 changes the HdfsSensor classes for the new HdfsHook. The biggest change is that it rewrites the HdfsSensor + HdfsSensorFolder (from contrib) sensors into a HdfsFileSensor and a HdfsFolderSensor class, which aim to retain the functionality of the previous classes. The main difference between the two sensors is that the former checks for files, whilst the latter checks for directories. In doing so, the HdfsFolderSensor also provides options for requiring directories to be (non-)empty, which is difficult to do if we were to keep a single HdfsSensor class. Note that this pulls the HdfsFolderSensor into `airflow.hooks`, effectively promoting the sensor from contrib. 

Besides this, the sensor classes now also accept a list of filter functions, which can filter a list of file/directory paths before the final check. This allows users to filter for files with a given minimum size, for example. 

I added a few deprecation utility classes/functions to help keep the re-written classes as backwards compatible as possible. However, if we move this to Airflow 2.0 I would consider removing these shims and simply breaking with the old interface, as adapting DAGs to these new sensors should be fairly straightforward. In that case, I would also argue for removing the `HdfsRegexSensor` class, which is largely superseded by the use of glob patterns in file/directory paths.

Another way to approach this would be to encourage folks to install Jupyter notebook/lab/etc. into a separate environment, and then have a new management command for installing a kernel for the current Django environment. This seems to follow the recommendation from the [IPython docs](https://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install/kernel_install.html#kernels-for-different-environments). I think the behavior would be similar to `python -m ipykernel install --user --name myvenv`, but with a kernel spec that has an `argv` similar to the current `shell_plus --notebook`.

FWIW, I have a repo for my base Jupyter environment, with instructions on how to add kernels for other environments: https://github.com/bhrutledge/jupyter-venv. The advantage of this approach is that it avoids cluttering up the Django environment with all of Jupyter's dependencies, and means you only have to have one notebook/lab instance running. Of course, the current approach of `shell_plus --notebook` is probably easier for people who haven't used Jupyter before.

Hmm, I think this might actually be expected behavior.

I don't see any watches being spun up for those paths in your log.  The path that you pass to to `query` has to be a watched root otherwise it won't know how to address your query.

The general best practice is to establish the watch before attempting to query.  This is the simplest way to do it, but is using the legacy `watch` command (more details on this below):

```
watchman watch $ROOT
watchman query ...
```

The mercurial `fsmonitor` extension uses a pattern like this, where it optimistically assumes that the watch is ready in order to shave off a few milliseconds in the common case:

```
result = client.query("query", hgroot, ...)
if result is error containing "not watched":
    client.query("watch", hgroot)
    result = client.query("query", hgroot, ...)
```

Both of the above examples use the legacy `watch` command.

Note that watchman has a watch aggregation feature and optional enforcement; we have this enabled at Facebook to prevent creating overlapping watches of very large directory trees.   https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/config.html#root_files and https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/config.html#enforce_root_files are settings that enable this.  When enabled, `watch` will fail unless you run it on the repo root.

(You can't easily enable this feature on windows at the moment because the default filename for the global configuration path is a unix path that cannot exist; I'll put up a diff to move this somewhere more reasonable this week).

The way to make this work correctly in all cases is to use `watch-project` (https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/cmd/watch-project.html) instead of `watch`.  It's not quite a drop-in replacement; `watch-project` will return the path to the watched root and an offset that you are expected to add to your query:

```
$ watchman watch-project C:/git-sdk-64/usr/src/git/t/trash directory.t2013-checkout-submodule
{
  "watch": "C:/git-sdk-64/usr/src/git",
  "relative_path": "t/trash directory.t2013-checkout-submodule"
}
```

Then issue a query that includes a `relative_root` parameter set to the `relative_path` field from the `watch-project` response (yes, the naming is slightly inconsistent!) and passes the `watch` field as the `root` in the query:

```
$ echo '["query", "C:/git-sdk-64/usr/src/git", { "since": 1497899747, "relative_root": "t/trash directory.t2013-checkout-submodule", "fields": ["name"], "expression": ["not", ["allof", ["since", 1497899747, "cclock"], ["not", "exists"]]] }]' | watchman -j
```

This is going to be a bit more fiddly to do in bash; it is "OK" to use `watch` as the use of `enforce_root_files` is not widespread.

> Could you explain a bit more as to why Django handles this case badly, and link to the upstream discussions/tickets about this? It seems like something we should encourage them to fix (even if only in parallel to us working around it in the meantime).

There are various discussions about this, and it is certainly a well-known issue. For example [1] and the linked issues. The underlying problem is that django handles cascading deletes by loading all the child objects to be deleted and deleting them in a way that causes any post-deletion code to be run. This is inherently slower than just asking the database to delete all the child objects when the parent disappears.

> Also, I'm wondering if a custom field type wrapping FlexibleForeignKey would be preferable compared to hardcoding this in migrations? I can't believe others aren't also hitting this, there may be prior art around we can use?

I agree others are hitting this, but I can't see prior art. I don't think that the Django custom field stuff is useful for what we need because it only seems to allow you to specify the datatype, not the full SQL used to construct the field in the first place. I would have to dig into the Django code to be sure however.

> Lastly, the way we've been doing cycle-data with the datasource tables was to manually delete the rows from the tables that had a foreign key on the parent table first. What are the disadvantages of just doing the same as that, but with the ORM this time?

Well it seems more complicated and potentially still slower; instead of letting the database propagate the deletion we have to manually do so. I suppose it will work if there is a reason to prefer that approach. What's your specific concern with this approach?

[1] https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/django-developers/FGDanrS9vlw

Hmmm, I just read through #55 again...

The issue discussed in it does not seem to be quite the same. Or rather, it is the same, but I'm not sure the identified problem is quite the right one: it was all about raising the wrong type of error.

Lets look at this example again:

```
class TestUser(DocType): 
    email = String(index="not_analyzed")
    pw_hash = String(index="not_analyzed")
    class Meta:
        index = "testing"
        doc_type = "test_users"
    @property
    def password(self):
        raise AttributeError('Password is not a readable attribute.')
    @password.setter
    def password(self, password):
        self.pw_hash = generate_password_hash(password)
```

And then:

```
>>> test_user = TestUser()
>>> test_user.password
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/Nicolas/my_project/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch_dsl-0.0.4.dev0-py3.3.egg/elasticsearch_dsl/utils.py", line 103, in __getattr__
KeyError: 'password'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/Nicolas/my_project/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch_dsl-0.0.4.dev0-py3.3.egg/elasticsearch_dsl/document.py", line 89, in __getattr__
  File "/Users/Nicolas/my_project/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch_dsl-0.0.4.dev0-py3.3.egg/elasticsearch_dsl/utils.py", line 399, in __getattr__
  File "/Users/Nicolas/my_project/flask/lib/python3.3/site-packages/elasticsearch_dsl-0.0.4.dev0-py3.3.egg/elasticsearch_dsl/utils.py", line 106, in __getattr__
AttributeError: 'TestUser' object has no attribute 'password'
```

And there is the ```AttributeError```. But not because of some mechanism that hides another type of error.

It is actually because ```@property``` is not doing its job:

```
>>> test_user.password = "test"
>>> test_user.password
'test'
```

To me it seems like the ```@property``` is completely ignored, which would be a BIG issue, even a deal breaker for me using elasticsearch-dsl-py. And I REALLY want to use it...

Yeah, I think it's a Two Generals' Problem. I guess we could include a list of running tasks in get_work rather than ping and have the scheduler re-send tasks that the worker doesn't know are supposed to be running.

There's also the problem where throwing any exception in the main worker thread while subprocesses are running will only kill that thread and result in the scheduler thinking all tasks are running until they all finish and the keep alive thread finally dies. This only happens to me with weird get_work failures right now (somehow returning None) but I don't see another generic way to handle this without killing the subprocesses.

The pinging solves both problems, although it does add extra delays by failing the tasks instead of marking them available for get_work again right away. I guess we could go straight to pending or at least keep it failed for less time.

That is still a small number of messages. You can be more or less guaranteed to have 10 messages every time if you have several thousand messages in the queue. Here's a little script to put 5000 messages in the queue:

```python
import boto3


sqs_url = QUEUE_URL
sqs = boto3.resource('sqs')
queue = sqs.Queue(sqs_url)


for i in range(500):
    messages = []
    for j in range(10):
        n = str(i * 10 + j)
        messages.append({
            "Id": n,
            "MessageBody": "Message %s" % n
        })
    queue.send_messages(Entries=messages)
```

After running that, I got 10 messages just about every time. You still won't get 10 if most of the messages are 'in flight'.

I figured out a work-around.  The original boto3 develop/boto3/resources/action.py "logging to much" issue still exists.
I cap the message portion to 16,000 bytes and then boto3 no longer logs something too long.

Example of solution:
---------------------
out_hdlr = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
# Cap message size so boto3 doesn't throw a secondary "OSError: [Errno 90] Message too long"
# stack (which is originally triggered by a large dynamodb write that fails).
formatter  = logging.Formatter(fmt='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message).16000s')
out_hdlr.setFormatter(formatter)
logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.addHandler(out_hdlr)

Hi @anthrotype ! Good eye, `shiv` is actually largely inspired by pex, which is a fantastic piece of software.

I'll expand on this bit of history as part of the doc overhaul requested in #10, but long story short is that we built `shiv` to address the invocation overhead that pex imposes.

Because pex leans heavily on `pkg_resources`, it's subject to substantial slowdown due to https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/510. Pex actually somewhat exacerbates the slowness by adding each dependency to sys.path individually. 

So you can think of `shiv` as a reimagining of pex without pkg_resources :)

Thank you @hhatto. Would you feel comfortable adding some narrative text to the documentation under [Precompiling](https://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/pyramid_chameleon/en/latest/#precompiling)? If not, I can add it later, but it would be good to include it with this PR. Here's my suggestion (line wrapping at 79 columns is not required in narrative documentation):

```rst
However compilation is relatively slow compared with execution, so the
first time a view is rendered may be very slow. To work around this
first request latency we offer a command line script to pre-compile
templates to python.
To further speed up the precompilation, use the ``--jobs <integer>`` option with an integer specifying the number of parallel jobs to run on a multiprocessor computer.
This is executed as follows:

.. code-block:: bash
    $ CHAMELEON_CACHE=/path/to/put/precompiled/templates \
        pyramid-chameleon-precompile --dir /path/to/look/for/templates \
        [--jobs <integer>]

With the ``--jobs`` option, the duration of compilation can be reduced by about one-third in projects with over 300 templates, as in this example using ``time``.

.. code-block:: bash

    $ time CHAMELEON_CACHE=~/tmp_chameleoncache pyramid-chameleon-precompile --dir ~/template_files --jobs 1
    INFO:root:Compiled 318 out of 318 found templates
    CHAMELEON_CACHE=~/tmp_chameleoncache pyramid-chameleon-precompile --dir   1  63.78s user 1.04s system 92% cpu 1:09.71 total
    $ rm -rf ~/tmp_chameleoncache/*
    $ time CHAMELEON_CACHE=~/tmp_chameleoncache pyramid-chameleon-precompile --dir ~/template_files --jobs 2
    INFO:root:Compiled 318 out of 318 found templates
    CHAMELEON_CACHE=~/tmp_chameleoncache pyramid-chameleon-precompile --dir   2  78.24s user 1.07s system 172% cpu 45.959 total
```

My initial proposal for a list of alert keywords is below. It's based on the actions you actually can do with alerts and influenced also by the existing set of keywords.

1. `Alert Should Be Present [expected_message=None]`: Existing keyword but changed argument name. Old functionality is OK, but could also return the actual alert message. Can also consider adding negative variant `Alert Should Not Be Present`.

2. `Handle Alert [action=ACCEPT]`: New keyword. By default accepts the alert (i.e. presses Ok), but allows dismissing it (i.e. pressing Cancel) by using like `action=DISMISS`, and could also support `action=LEAVE` or similar to leave the alert in place. Should return the alert message.

3. `Input Text Into Alert [text]`: Same as the current `Input Text Into Prompt`.

For my eyes, it is most easiest to do it like this:
```| ${width} | ${height} = | Get Element Size | locator=some_locator_here |```

Instead of doing it like this:
```
| ${locator} = | Set Variable | locator=some_locator_here |
| ${width} = | Get Element Width | ${locator} |
| ${height} = | Get Element Height | ${locator} |
```
or this:
```
| ${width} = | Get Element Width | locator=some_locator_here |
| ${height} = | Get Element Height | locator=some_locator_here |
```
Either one will copy/paste the _locator_ two times and copy/paste should be avoided in all times. Or one puts the _locator_ in a variable and then you have three keywords instead of one. Also there is small performance penalty, the DOM is searched two times, if you one uses two separate keywords. Some updates on investigation. So `update <package>` in (implementation-wise) the same as `install <package> --selective-upgrade`, which would set `keep_outdated` to `True`. Since the package is already installed in this case, the locking mechanism would elect to keep the old version, instead of fetching the latest. So either the behaviour is correct, or we should tweak the implementation to do different things in `update` from `install --selective-upgrade`.

I think it only makes sense in the context where `Pipfile` is being used to manage local development environments, but *not* production builds or deployments.

You're not especially likely to do that in a SaaS context (or if you are, then exporting `Pipfile` to a flat requirements file is likely to work), but that's not my use case: my use case is getting to a point where developers can combine "git clone" with "pipenv shell", and getting everything they need to work on the charger software, including the dependencies that pylint et al need in order to make proper sense of any Python code.

I don't want to pull those dependency lists directly into Pipfile, because there are other build processes that need them to be where they are.

That said, if this were to be declared out of scope for `pipenv` itself, then I'd likely just pull it into scope for wainwright, and treat the `Pipfile` contents as just another source for a later `pip-compile` and `pip-sync` call (targeting the `pipenv` managed venv).

In an earlier version of setuptools, we started using tox rather than pytest-runner as the preferred test runner for setuptools. In 93d8715bff, I removed the alias that made `setup.py test` invoke pytest-runner. As a result, `setup.py test` is now invoking unittest, which is not supported. I recommend you adapt your tests to use tox (following the technique that the .travis.yml or appveyor.yml files employ). If you wish to continue using pytest-runner, you're welcome to do so, but you'll want to invoke `setup.py pytest` rather than rely on the alias that I would prefer to leave removed so as not to incentivize use of that technique.

I just merged master to update the Travis results.

* Specifying "*" in the attribute list is explicitly supported in RFC4511, so that seems like a good change.

* The change you made to correct the recursion limit-- something doesn't seem right to me.  The `subtree()` method returns a Deferred.  This suggests to me that the function might block.  Your change calls the children `subtree()` methods iteratively and *synchronously*.  That means if it does block, the whole server will be blocking.

I don't think you ran into this because the 2 existing DIT models, `inmemory.py` and `ldiftree.py`, don't block when they call their `children()` method.  However, I could imagine a DIT model based on a database backend that *could* block to fetch children.

I do think the existing code needs to be corrected.  If `children()` were asynchronous, it wouldn't make sense to wait for the result of each child before moving on to the next.  Maybe something like a DeferredList would be appropriate?

Hi @superbobry,

Yesterday evening, I rewrote half of my ``stream`` class and ended up with this: https://github.com/jonathanslenders/pymux/blob/master/pymux/stream.py#L56

The result is about 10x faster. (Pymux as a whole became at least 3x faster because of this.)

The problem with the original code is that many things happen for every single character that we receive from the process. Normally, in Python we don't like to do string manipulations at the character level, because that is slow. So, if we have to do, we have to make this part very efficient.

- There was a feed and a consume function. This doesn't make sense because every external call to "consume" could as well have gone to "feed". So better place the for-loop in-line and don't do type checking twice.
- The lookup in ``self.handlers`` is expensive. Way too expensive to do for every single character. Better than setting a ``self.state`` string, would be to set a ``self.current_handler`` callable.
- Actually all attribute lookups are expensive. (The ``.`` operator is expensive.) Use the "dis" module to analyse.
- ``getattr`` and ``hasattr`` are slow.
- I didn't need multiple listeners, so I optimized my code to have just one listener.

What I did was putting all the state in a coroutine, a generator that I feed one character at a time. All variables that are used are locals in that function, so the lookup will be very fast. Calls to dispatch are optimized. ``dispatch`` is even avoided when we can call the function directly. Further, we don't handle any exceptions. Thanks to defaultdict, we can't have exceptions in this coroutine itself, and we won't hide exceptions from the stream class. The function is organized in a way that we don't need the calls to ``reset``. (Everything that ``reset`` did was setting variables that are know initialized at the right point in the coroutine.)

I know that this function is longer. But it's manageable, and IMHO worth it, because of the performance benefit.

I'm not sure when I will have time to create pull requests for Pyte, but feel free to copy the useful parts.

It's an illusion. :)

The loop doesn't actually wait for messages. When an HTTP request arrives carrying WebSocket events, the django-grip middleware parses all the events in the request body and puts them in a queue inside of the wscontext object. So when you call recv() all you're really doing is popping from a memory queue, not blocking on a network read. Similarly, when you call send(), all you're doing is queuing outbound events. When the view method completes, the post processing part of the django-grip middleware converts the send queue into an HTTP response to send back to Pushpin.

I'd like to reopen this issue for two reasons:

1. All the tests for [Python 2.7 using pickle failed](https://travis-ci.org/zopefoundation/ZEO/builds/358513676) after #108 was merged. There are a bunch of `ClientDisconnected: ('tpc_vote', 'on a disconnected transaction’)` in the output, which is what we're seeing on our clients. Note that the 2.7 test using the msgpack protocol *passed*.
2. @cutz followed the `ImportError` down, and it appears to be the case that `server_find_global` simply won't allow `zodbpickle.binary` to be loaded, directly raising the `ImportError` we see above.

So it looks like an additional needed fix is to modify `server_find_global` to allow `zodbpickle` as a module name.

We're provoking the warning as a side-effect of test cases that ensure we can't put float values in X[LI]BTree objects.

```
  File "//BTrees/BTrees/tests/common.py", line 1805, in _insertfloatraises
    self._makeOne().insert(1.4)
  File "//BTrees/BTrees/_base.py", line 572, in add
    return self._set(self._to_key(key))[0]
  File "//BTrees/BTrees/_base.py", line 1507, in to_int
    if not int_unpack(int_pack(v))[0] == v: #pragma: no cover
DeprecationWarning: integer argument expected, got float
```

On Python 3, that immediately raises a `struct.error`. 

Objects of types with `__index__` methods are processed successfully on both versions. Python 3 errors on things with just `__int__` while Python 2 raises the same warning.

Could we replace the pack/unpack/equality check with something like:
```
def to_int(v):
   try:
      return pack('i', operator.index(v))
   except struct_error:
      raise TypeError()
```

That looks to be pretty comparable to what the C code does using `PyLong_AsLongLongAndOverflow` and raising if there's overflow. operator.index raises if the object doesn't implement `__index__` or `__index__` doesn't return an int (or long on Py2).

I have some reservations about this. On the technical side it is simply a very different way to do things  that also needs some other changes - you need to not only support multiple recipes in one file, but give them all a name manually and probably load them in a key-value store when toolchain.py is run, because otherwise you don't know what recipes are available and can't access a specific one - for instance, your `hostpython_recipe = Recipe.get_recipes('hostpython2', self.ctx)[0]` won't work once there are multiple recipes in one file, because you can't hardcode the [0].

The current way of doing things is motivated largely by the idea that actually there aren't very many related recipes (and even e.g. the python recipes aren't that interrelated), that they are rarely modified, and that they really are recipes philosophically rather than python objects. That is, they will largely be interacted with by quickly looking at individual recipes via the filesystem, not by loading them in different ways with code, so making the overall set obvious is more valuable than creating conceptual relationships for developers. At least, these are my motivations for keeping it simple like this, I can't speak for sure why tito chose this structure.

I'm not necessarily averse to going your way and constructing a big list of recipes when toolchain.py is run (this would also make it obvious how to support user-loaded recipes, whereas right now at least one new thing is necessary to do that), but I'm not convinced right now it's actually a better way. I guess  I suspect I'm also instinctively conservative about it, so I'm open to changing my mind.

This is a to me a clearer example that demonstrates the problem, as it shows the scoping:

    from numexpr.necompiler import _names_cache, _numexpr_cache, _numexpr_last
    import numexpr as ne
    import numpy as np
    import sys, gc

    def memory_leak():
        a = np.arange(10)

        def good():
            print('good: Pre f_locals call', sys.getrefcount(a))
            ldict = sys._getframe(1).f_locals
            print('good: Post f_locals call', sys.getrefcount(a))
            ldict.clear()
            print('good: cleared ldict', sys.getrefcount(a))

        def bad():
            print('Pre f_locals call', sys.getrefcount(a))
            ldict = sys._getframe(1).f_locals
            print('Post f_locals call', sys.getrefcount(a))
            
        good()
        print('Exited scope of good()', sys.getrefcount(a))
        gc.collect()
        print('Collected garbage', sys.getrefcount(a))

        print('='*78)
        bad()
        print('Exited scope of bad()', sys.getrefcount(a))
        gc.collect()
        print('Collected garbage', sys.getrefcount(a))

    memory_leak()

So yes I see now that `sys._getframe({n>0}).f_locals` may be undefined behavior for CPython.

> My guess was right. Even without knowing the format, it is easy to improve compression ratio.  Step 1: split odd and even bytes, e.g. with python script

Weird approach but it gave good results.  The file is a bit of a mish-mash between float and integer data.  It will exhibit a 32-bit alignment pattern as it is filled mostly with 32 bit floats and 32 bit floats with some exceptions.

As some asked, a trimesh / polymesh format is for representing piece-wise linear objects in 3D.  This format is supports both trimeshes and polygon meshes:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triangle_mesh
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polygon_mesh

It is composed of a list of 3D positions (X,Y,Z as 32 bit floats triples), and then a list of integers which state how those points are connected to form triangles or polygons, (one list of integers which states how many points per face/polygon, and then another list of those indices for each face) and then it contains normals (more XYZ 32 bit float triples) and UVs (UV 32 bit float pairs).  This format actually will change the precision of the integers from 8 to 16 to 32 bit depending upon their required maximum values, but I believe this files is mostly 32 bit integers because it is a large polygon mesh.

FYI, here's how the geographiclib package handles bad values.

* All arguments are expected to be numeric.  However this is not
  explicitly checked.  Instead the user will get some complaint from the
  bowels of the code.

* Exact range reduction is done on longitudes and azimuths.  This means
  that any finite values are OK.  Inf becomes NaN.

* Distances are unrestricted.

* Latitudes must lie in [-90,90].  Latitudes outside this range are
  **silently** turned into NaNs.

* NaNs and Infs progagate through the code as expected.  (Actually this
  isn't quite true since with python `math.sin(float("inf"))` ->
  `math domain error`.  I'll fix this.)

The expectation is that wacky values should be handled by some outer
user-facing layer.

One advantage of NFF (one file per column) is that columns can be added and deleted very easily and robustly. It's easy and robust because there is no file format to navigate, hence the name NFF. Sharding by directory enables historical data to be locked down read-only while a new directory is added safely for each new day of data (50GB/day in my experience). Such datasets are not moved around from machine to machine; it's not whether it's multiple files or one big file that prevents people from transferring the data, but the shear size of the data.   For small datasets, a tar file is no different from one big file.

What seems to be driving this change is data with very large number of columns (>10,000). Points 2-5 are the same point about that. But really, are very very wide datasets like this needed to be out-of-memory?  I'm more used to database storage where data is stored in normal format : tall and skinny.  I doubt you would ever see a (reasonable) SQL database containing a table of more than 1000 columns.  It's more common to add or remove columns from an existing very large database (many billions of rows, under 10,000 columns) and at risky times like that it's really advantageous to have no-format.

If it's going to be one-big-file, why (to predict comments from community) "yet another different proprietary format from a startup that might disappear one day, and not Arrow or Parquet"?
