Not normally.  In a regular FITS header, there are also CRPIX1, CRPIX2.  IIRC, these are the (x,y) coordinates that correspond to the sky coordinates given by CRVAL1, CRVAL2.

The origin (x,y) = (0,0) is normally the origin of the chip coordinate system.  Typically, slightly off the lower left corner, since the center of the lower left pixel is normally defined as (1,1).

If you have an image which has both a normal fits header and also a description using cameraGeom, you can probably figure out the appropriate conventions by comparing what values for (x,y) cameraGeom uses vs what ds9 shows.  I did a lot of that kind of comparison in writing the other wcs classes.  The unit tests in test_wcs.py reflect this, comparing the values we get for various wcs back ends with what ds9 showed for a few choice (x,y) positions for a number of different WCS types.

Also, to chime in here, it would worry me to have default calculations be good to only 1 decimal place for some types and more for others.  It’s something that could catch a user by surprise.  Can you make the default to be accurate and slow, then allow people to speed it up if they are willing to sacrifice accuracy?

I wouldn't be at all surprised if InterpolatedImage is just the wrong tool for the job when all you care about is 2d interpolation of some function.  The InterpolatedImage class has a lot of stuff designed to make sure the FFT comes out correctly without aliasing and in particular without leading to artifacts when the object is sheared.  These aren't things you care about for just interpolating a function between samples, so it's very possible that those features are just slowing you down and not providing any value.

Yeah, same issue as @Naatan. The real problem for me is that I'm using npm to download and use font-awesome. I can't just add some text file into the module folder because of we have the links. It's not the right way. npm is part of a deploy process, it's not possible to proceed *adding files by hand* for every deploy. Furthermore, licenses are different for different contents, and that, legally, is a bit painful, because you need to add the licence in the right folder only, avoiding misundestanding:

- `/fonts` folder needs http://scripts.sil.org/OFL
- all that remains use MIT

My company would like to embed font-awesome in the project that sells, but actually it can't because of licenses. The only way to add text licenses is to create a tool that downloads them and put wherever they should placed: no way.

We have two different contracts here.
 1. Writes on master should be allowed only if we have a synchronous replica
 2. Patroni first should pick and set one replica as a synchronous and only after write this information to DCS

Patroni is responsible for the first contract only. It can't block writes on the master until information is exposed to DCS.

When we have some replicas streaming from the master - everything is all right, old algorithm works (Patroni should pick one of the replicas, update postgresql.conf and expose it's name to DCS)

Interesting things are happening when we have a corner case: if the synchronous mode is on and there are no replicas connected, Patroni removes name of old synchronous replica from DCS and removes `synchronous_standby_names` from postgresql.conf

For this corner case scenario (when strict mode is on) I propose to set `synchronous_standby_names`  to `'*'` instead of `dummy_host`.

It would be set to `'*'` only until some replica has connected. As soon it happened - Patroni will pick it up and change `synchronous_standby_names` from `'*'` to an actual replica name.

First of all you need to figure out why it is happening.
I envision following possibilities:
1) Huge value of `wal_keep_segments` on replica.
2) There are some "staled" replication slots on replica.

In order to diagnose it properly please connect to the replica with `psql` and run following:
1) `show wal_keep_segments` -- if the value is big (unlikely), this is the reason, just fix the config and restart
2) `SELECT * FROM pg_replication_slots` -- if there are some replication slots exist, they will prevent wal segments from cleaning up. Try to drop them with `SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('<slot_name>');`, it should help.

The only question would be, what names do these slots have? (Are the names matching with the names of other cluster nodes?)

I dug into this a little bit to refresh my memory. I couldn't remember if it was a problem with my branch, or also on master.
It turns out that form parameters won't be rejected based on the "consumes" part of the operation.
For example, the spec below still accepts the form parameters, even though `consumes = ["application/json"]`.
```yaml
  /test-formData-param:
    post:
      summary: Test formData parameter
      operationId: fakeapi.hello.test_formdata_param
      consumes:
        - application/json  # connexion assumes application/json if this is blank
      # - application/x-www-form-urlencoded
      parameters:
        - name: formData
          type: string
          in: formData
          required: true
      responses:
        200:
          description: OK
```
The client still sends the correct content type alongside the form data, but the spec doesn't specify that the endpoint consumes form content types, and connexion isn't strict - it let's the form data through.

It's an oddity of the swagger 2.0 spec that you can specify `in: formData` separately from the `consumes` block. This is fixed in the 3.0 spec with the introduction of `requestBody`. The existing behavior may actually make sense for the 2.0 spec - I'm not sure. If you were to reject formData parameter if the `consumes` block didn't match, that should/would probably be in a spec validator. What do you think?

You can reproduce this by running:
```bash
cd tests
PYTHONPATH=$PWD connexion run fixturesimple/swagger.yaml -p 9090
# in another terminal...
curl -X POST -F "formData=somedata" http://localhost:9090/v1.0/test-formData-param
```

Either way, your change looks good to me!

I compared the `.c` files generated by Cython on both Linux and Windows and they're identical except for a string containing the path to the PYX source used to generate the `.c` file. So I don't think there'll be any platform specific issues.

If there are issues with `setup_requires` then we can bypass it by distributing the `.c` source files with pyrobuf and using code something like the following to build the list and util modules:

```python
try:
    from Cython.Build import cythonize

    extension_modules = cythonize([os.path.join('pyrobuf', 'src', '*.pyx')],
                                   include_path=[os.path.join('pyrobuf', 'src')])
except ImportError:
    extension_modules = [Extension(os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(src))[0], [src])
                         for src in glob.glob(os.path.join('pyrobuf', 'src', 'pyrobuf_*.c'))]
```

and then changing the setup call to

```python
setup(
    name="pyrobuf",
    version=VERSION,
    packages=find_packages(),
    include_package_data=True,
    cmdclass={'install': install,
              'generate_list': GenerateList,
              'list_and_util': ListAndUtil},
    entry_points={
        'console_scripts': ['pyrobuf = pyrobuf.__main__:main']
    },
    description='A Cython based protobuf compiler',
    long_description=open(os.path.join(HERE, 'README.md')).read(),
    url='https://github.com/appnexus/pyrobuf',
    author='AppNexus',
    install_requires=['jinja2', 'cython >= 0.23'],
    ext_modules=extension_modules
)
```

This would include the list and util modules with pyrobuf, so a separate list-and-util module would not be necessary.

Hmm, thinking more about that, I think there is actually some hard limitation to run several tests in a single run.

Let's consider a tax and benefit systems with 3 variables `A`, `B` and `C`, with the following dependencies:
```A-->B-->C```
A depends on B, and B depends on C.


Let's consider the following tests:

```yaml
- name: "I - Check A formula"
  input_variables:
    B:
      2018-07: 10
  output_variables:
    A:
      2018-07: 20

- name: "II - Check B formula"
  input_variables:
    C:
      2018-07: 10
  output_variables:
    B:
      2018-07: 20

- name: "III - End to end test"
  input_variables:
    C:
      2018-07: 10
  output_variables:
    A:
      2018-07: 40
```

First, let's try to merge tests `I` and `II`. The input variables of the resulting single simulation would be `B` and `C`. But `B` is also an output of test `II`!
As `B`'s value will be set as an input, its formula won't be used, and test `II` won't run properly.

Conclusion: _If a variable is both an input of a test and an output of another test, then these tests can't run at the same time._

More subtle, let's consider merging `I` and `III`. They don't fall in the previous category. But we would run into the same issue anyway: `B` is an input variable of the resulting single simulation, so its formula won't be used. But that will prevent test `III` from running properly, as `A` depends on `B`.

Conclusion: _If a variable `B` is an intermediate dependency of a variable `A`, then we can't merge a test where `B` is an input and `A` an output_. By "intermediate dependency" I mean a dependency that has a formula.


To be more accurate we would have to take periods into account:

- _If a couple variable-period is both an input of a test and an output of another test, then these tests can't run at the same time._
- _If a variable-period couple `(B, p1)` is an intermediate dependency of a variable-period couple `(A, p2)`, then we can't merge a test where `(B, p1)` is an input and `(A, p2)` an output_.

We just started using this library and everytime we reindex our ranking formula is still kept but the synonyms and query rules get wiped. We created a hotfix specific to our use case that export the query rules and synonyms, reindex then import them again. The code is below. Hope this helps
```python
from algoliasearch_django.management.commands.algolia_reindex import Command as AlgoliaCommand
from algoliasearch import algoliasearch
from shops.index import ProductIndex
from django.conf import settings


class Command(AlgoliaCommand):
    help = 'Reindex all models to Algolia with Feedmee fixes'

    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        # FEEDMEE fix, export then import synonyms and queryrules
        client = algoliasearch.Client(settings.ALGOLIA["APPLICATION_ID"],
                                      settings.ALGOLIA["API_KEY"])
        index = client.init_index(settings.ALGOLIA["NON_REPLICA"])
        rules = []
        synonyms = []
        for r in index.iter_rules():
            rules.append(r)
        for s in index.iter_synonyms():
            synonyms.append(s)
        print("Found rules: {}".format(rules))
        print("Found synonyms: {}".format(synonyms))
        # the normal command
        super(Command, self).handle(*args, **options)
        # save them
        response = index.batch_rules(rules, forward_to_replicas=True)
        index.wait_task(response['taskID'])
        print("Saved rules with response: {}".format(response))
        response = index.batch_synonyms(synonyms, forward_to_replicas=True)
        index.wait_task(response['taskID'])
        print("Saved synonyms with response: {}".format(response))
```

class RequestSerialiser(object):
    """
    Utility class to proxy request from mock to boss.

    It is used to serialise requests as JSON data.
    """
    def __init__(self, path, request):
        if request.query_string:
            path = "{0}?{1}".format(path, request.query_string)

        self.body = binary_to_ascii(request.body.read())
        self.headers = to_dict(request.headers)
        self.method = request.method
        self.url = path
        self.GET = request.GET.dict
        self.POST = request.POST.dict
        self.rule = "{0} {1}".format(request.method, path)

    def serialize(self):
        data = {
            'body': self.body,
            'headers': self.headers,
            'method': self.method,
            'url': self.url,
            'GET': self.GET,
            'POST': self.POST,
            'rule': self.rule,
        }
        return json.dumps(data)

```
class BreakSetup(TestCase):
    called = False

    def setUp(self):
        if self.called:
            return
        super(BreakSetup, self).setUp()

    def test_nil(self):
        pass

    def tearDown(self):
        if self.called:
            super(BreakSetup, self).tearDown()
        self.__class__.called = True

```
This simulates a test that fails to upcall in setup if some global state is borked, and fails to call tearDown at all. I believe if run twice with your patch the first time it will trigger the ValueError, and then the second time when it does not upcall in setUp, it won't trigger an error even though its now buggy. I've seen this sort of thing dealing with e.g. locked databases, so I  think its important to be correct here. I suspect its in the pathological space where hypothesis would be a better fit than entire-space-checking, but this sort of stateful interaction is probably something we need coverage on too.

tl;dr, the finally needs to guard the __setup_called and __teardown_called resetting, only. Or move it to the start of run() or something.

Regarding going through Astropy's general I/O framework, @embray, do you mean via `NDIOMixin.write()`? Or some `WCS.write()` method that modifies an extension header (perhaps having to re-write the whole file each time)? Actually, your `read()` example suggests the latter. I think it's important to have an API for encoding the FITS & ASDF headers in memory, since Astropy I/O currently deals only with a single set of FITS extensions, whereas we need to be able to write files containing some number of NDData instances (each with a wcs attribute), which currently requires a 3rd-party package, while modifying an existing thing on disk N times is at best inefficient. I would see the wcs object as something associated with an NDData instance (like general meta-data), more than a thing that you want to read & manipulate separately. I would like to be able to hook into a "write this  this gwcs object to this FITS/ASDF header object" in my own `write()` method.

It's just due diligence. We can't track down and ask everyone who is using `gcloud` to "update, pretty please". What we can do is that if someone files a bug or support request it will be clearly communicated in their logs that the library is no longer supported.

This is a similar to someone using an outdated version of `google-cloud` - our first support impulse would be to tell them to update and see if it helps. The story is the same here, but we're just being extra explicit that this isn't supported and leaving a trail of auditability.

Also, it's very likely that users who are still on `gcloud` won't update from whatever version they're currently using. So even if you did the nuclear option of deploying a broken package, they still probably wouldn't notice.

Second, I would avoid installing into your "base" Python's `site-packages`, and instead use a virtualenv.

In general, supporting and testing EOL versions is becoming increasingly difficult as other packages and tools drop them. 

EOL Pythons (<=2.6, 3.0-3.3) no longer receive security updates, or any updates, from the core Python team. It is possible to upgrade Python on Linux to a supported version for those who care about security. Those who don't can pin to an older version.

Dropping old ones, particularly Python 2.6 (which has been EOL for over 4 years) and 3.2 (2 years), allows code to be updated to use more modern features of Python.

So after doing a little more research, I'm convinced I was mistaken on the purpose of `crti.o`. While the documentation on any of this is of course severely lacking, I think I had misconstrued mentions of "global constructors" or "init" as strictly referring to a main function, and therefore that `crti.o` would be necessary specifically for an executable, but not a shared library. My vague half-understanding as of now is that `crti.o` provides hooks which are necessary for any ELF object -- since this appears to be true for any shared object file anyone has ever distributed, my concern that this would somehow link the produced shared object to the specific version of libc providing `crti.o` on the host which produces the shared library seems unfounded, or workaroundable.

I *believe* part of the reason for my confusion is that the "init" facility provided by `crti.o` is used in executables for linking up the `main` method to executable invocation (**this is a complete guess**). Another alternative interpretation is that `crti.o` is necessary regardless, and hooking up the `main` method is totally separate. The resources that led to me to this tentative conclusion are [this page on the OSDev Wiki](https://wiki.osdev.org/Creating_a_C_Library#crtbegin.o.2C_crtend.o.2C_crti.o.2C_and_crtn.o) (which does not provide much information about what `crti.o` is for) and [this stackoverflow response](https://stackoverflow.com/a/15180338/2518889) (which does).

(*this information should be cataloged in an issue somewhere, probably, or maybe a README in the native backend. i'll take responsibility for doing that*)

Regardless of the correct interpretation, it now seems (more) clear that `crti.o` *is* indeed necessary for creating shared object files, so I will now attempt to figure out why it's not being found. As mentioned above, parsing the output of `g++ -print-search-dirs` actually seems to be finding where `crti.o` *should* be on travis -- but obviously this isn't working, so I will figure out why that is happening. This error appears to be the only thing blocking #6205 as well, so this planned hotfix `1.9.0rc2` should be good to go after this is figured out.

I expect that the issue is related to {{consolidate-classpath}} not properly invalidating though. In the case of bundle, (afaik), the tasks involved in getting resources into jars are: `resources.prepare` (which puts them on `runtime_classpath`), `bundle.consolidate-classpath` (which consumes `runtime_classpath` to create `consolidated_classpath`) and then finally the `bundle.jvm` goal which consumes the `consolidated_classpath`.

The way to confirm would be to check that you do actually see the resources prepared into the `.pants.d/resources/prepare/current` directory, and then _not_ getting cloned into the taskdir for consolidate-classpath.

Doing a custom domain in serverless is currently a big hack. With that said...

A BasePathMapping can be used but this will create a circular dependency upon deletion and the stack will not delete. To fix that include the DependsOn: ServerlessFunction directive. Then when the delete goes forward the failures can be ignored and then deleted manually.

Also the custom domain name should already be created. See http://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/how-to-custom-domains.html#how-to-custom-domains-console

```
  BasePathMapping:
    DependsOn: ServerlessFunction
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::BasePathMapping
    Properties:
      DomainName: "example.com"
      RestApiId:
        Ref: ServerlessRestApi
      Stage:
        Ref: ServerlessRestApiProdStage
```

Let's go a littler deeper into how we should be going about this. If we can land on a schema design here, I would be more than happy to get it prioritized as soon as I can. 

I see more or less two options. If you can think of others that would be useful please let me know. 

Option 1: 
Add a flag key to each relevant resource. This would need to be added to both the Event sources for API and the `AWS::Serverless::Api` type. There may be other resources or events that need something similar as well. So this option would mean we would need to duplicate all the work across all of them (to whatever they would be).
```
LambdaFunction:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
  Properties:
    ...
    Event:
      Type: API
      Properties:
        ...
        DisableTestPermissions: True
```

Option 2:
We can add some kind of 'Global' Flagging system. The idea behind this would to reduce the duplication across all Event Types and Resources and have a one stop shop for 'system' flags (something like compile flags might be a good way to think about this). This one isn't really fleshed out to me but I like the option more. Something like the below I think could be really powerful for not only this issue but possibly others as well.
```
Globals:
  SAMFlags:
    IsProduction: True
...
Resources:
  ...
```
I think it makes sense to put something like this into the globals section, but would love to here thoughts.

Copying from [#578](https://github.com/SoCo/SoCo/issues/578#issuecomment-370867259) @relevitt

----

#602 

- Allow the user a choice in how the event listener is implemented. The default is for the event listener to use the requests library and run in a thread. This update allows the user to run the event listener using the twisted.internet library, by setting the config.EVENTS_MODULE module to point to the events_twisted module. See the example in events_twisted.

- Stop the event listener when the last active subscription is unsubscribed.

- Raise SoCoException on an attempt to subscribe a subscription more than once (use Subscription.renew() instead).

- Allow an optional `strict` parameter for Subscription.subscribe(), Subscription.renew() and Subscription.unsubscribe(). If set to False, Exceptions will be logged rather than raised. Default: True

- Upon autorenewal, call Subscription.renew() with the `strict` flag set to False, so that any Exception is logged, not raised. This is because there is no calling code to catch an Exception.

- Provide for calling code to set `Subscription.auto_renew_fail` to refer to a callback function. If an Exception occurs upon autorenewal, this callback will be called with the Exception as the sole parameter.

- If an Exception occurs upon Subscription.subscribe() or Subscription.renew(), cancel the subscription, unless the Exception was a SoCoException on Subscription.subscribe(). For example, if an Exception occurs because the network went down, the subscription will be cancelled.

- Use a threading lock with Subscription.subscribe(), Subscription.renew() and Subscription.unsubscribe(), because autorenewal occurs from a thread.

Just a high-level question first, without having looked at the code yet: After moving the content, we don't have a systematic cross-platform way to persist the path change so that Kolibri will look to the new location, do we? So once they move it, they need to do some other configuration on their own, which we need to make sure they know. Right now, changing the content directory requires creating a custom settings file and then referencing it every time you start Kolibri (or in an environment variable). And with the PEX, it's not actually even technically possible since it can't reference external Python paths to find the settings module. This is a great example of why things like the content directory should be specified in some other sort of config file, rather than in a Django settings file. The main difference is that we have the Hindi translations for the labels, which are translated as 'blue part' rather than just 'blue', which makes them overly long.

In addition, there is an issue with the way the text is rendered that is internal to MathJax, the renderer that the exercises renderer uses (KA have their own library KATeX that tries to render it, but it fails with non-Western character sets, such as Hindi).

Relevant issues on MathJax and KATeX:

https://github.com/mathjax/MathJax/issues/474#issuecomment-38324717
https://github.com/Khan/KaTeX/issues/729

The only thing that can be fixed here (but again, not internally to Kolibri), is for the Hindi translations to be shortened just to 'blue' etc. rather than 'blue part'.

So the issue happens because the code that checks if there's an existing article with given slug, uses the current language instead of the language the article is being created on.

As far as I can tell there is nothing in the RFCs that state a BGP session should always support IPv4/unicast.  If only want to support say ipv6/unicast or ipv4/flowspec i should be able to without having ipv4/unicast tossed in as well. Also both juniper and cisco routers that I am familiar with are perfectly fine with not advertising support for ipv4/unicast.
Can I close this issue ?

In our use case, we use the state messages to indicate to our RIB processor that a BGP peer session has reset. Our child process is just a message recorder/queuer. If it restarts, we actually *don't* want a new state message, as that would look (to us) like a peer reset in the message stream, when in fact that did not happen.

In contrast to your situation, our ExaBGP child process is effectively stateless - we derive state from the message stream elsewhere.

Maybe the solution could be an optional config flag on the process, to send the latest/current state message on process restart/startup?

For the indirection (`>>`)to be understood as part of the command, you need to pass it as an argument to `sh -c`, like so: `sh -c '/bin/echo test1 teip >> test.txt'`. This applies to the docker CLI and docker-py.

```python
from docker import Client
cli = Client(base_url='unix://var/run/docker.sock')
exec_instance=cli.exec_create(container='hello',cmd="sh -c '/bin/echo \'test1 teip\'>>/test.txt'",user='root',tty=True)
cli.exec_start(exec_id, stream=True,detach=False)

```

I did a bit of digging and here's what I found.

The error you're seeing is happening from the `compute_drift()` function in `motion.py`. This function calculates the difference in position, delta x/delta y, between frames for a given particle. However, there's some clean-up that has to happen, since the x and y columns now hold delta x and delta y, but the frame and particle columns hold delta frame and delta particle. So, the original frame number and particle number has to be restored. That's what `reset_index()` is doing when it crashes. 

This error happens when Python expects a string but sees an integer instead (for example, trying to add a string and an integer). It happens [here in v 0.20.1](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/e346c663cf76186c22f4d3b703461b1b60db280f/pandas/core/frame.py#L3055) in the pandas source. That line checks if an integer i is in a string label. **This is likely a bug in pandas**. If you look at the source for the current version of pandas, v 0.21.0, the equivalent location [no longer has that check](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/81372093f1fdc0c07e4b45ba0f47b0360fabd405/pandas/core/frame.py#L3377). Try updating your pandas to version 0.21 and see if that fixes the issue.

If upgrading pandas does not fix the issue, please post the full code that you're using to generate `t1` and that causes the crash. If it's long, or if it requires external data, you can always use a [Gist](https://gist.github.com/) and include a link to the Gist.

Interestingly, the `compute_drift()` method trackpy versions 0.3.2 and 0.3.3 both call the `reset_index()` function, but the current master branch has re-factored the way this is done. [Here is the method compute_drift() in version 0.3.2](https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/blob/c309b83296c720b8b9fd9633259c2b9ea6740eeb/trackpy/motion.py#L257-L272), and [here is the method compute_drift() in the master branch](https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/blob/master/trackpy/motion.py#L263-L281) for comparison. So, another option to try and fix this would be to update your trackpy by building it from the master branch. (I've noticed a substantial increase in speed using the numba engine by building from the trackpy master branch, compared with the version of trackpy available through pip, so this may be worth trying anyway.)

For the record, [here](https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/commit/8590a6d8b4f21ed1fdc33183435334fb202ae1ee#diff-d63608fc47e191876c0f7677b166f389) is the commit where the `reset_index()` function calls were introduced to `compute_drift()` (Feb 2016).

I recently found another important situation where jinja2 configuration will be helpful: conda-build version 1.8.2 on Windows silently adds the default compiler to the PATH, unless this is explicitly overridden in the recipe by:
```
build:
  msvc_compiler: vc11  # request a specific compiler
```
Apart from the question if conda's default behavior is desirable (it took me several hours of debugging to find out why conda used inconsistent compiler settings -- the errors were really weired), the compiler version should not be hard-coded in the recipe. Instead, one might use a jinja2 variable:
```
build:
  msvc_compiler: {{compiler.version}}  # [win]
```

WARNING conda.lock:touch(53): Failed to create lock, do not run conda in parallel processes [errno 13]
WARNING conda.lock:touch(53): Failed to create lock, do not run conda in parallel processes [errno 13]

This is a regression from 4.1.x, please fix.

    conda clean -i

> What is the point to have aws key and secret options in the config if Curator is not compatible with AWS ES at all?

They were added before Curator 4 was released, as part of some feature adding being done by others.  Having no way to properly mock the calls, or to do proper integration testing, I merged pull requests submitted by others after they reported a functional outcome.  The `get_client` method was carried over from 3.x with little modification, so that is partly why those options persist, even if they've been renamed in places.

>  you decided for some reason to make optional API required and you refuse to support the way Curator 3 worked?

The /_cluster/state/metadata endpoint is not optional for Curator 4.  Curator 4 is a major version release, and in many ways is a full API rewrite.  So many of the things that Curator 3 did were suboptimal, and frequently repeated calls that didn't need to be repeated, if data was stored once.  As stated in a previous comment on this ticket, when Curator 4 was being developed, there was no such thing as AWS ES 2.x, so I had no way of knowing that Amazon would release a 2.x version, nor that if they did that it still would not support the metadata endpoint.  I developed for the unaltered, open-source releases of Elasticsearch, with the 2.x APIs and 5.x APIs in mind.  Another important note is that earlier versions of Curator 3 did not support AWS ES 1.x either, for the same reason.  A user submitted a method that would pull some necessary details using the _cat API, and thereby make Curator work (for that call) with AWS ES.  #717 has some interesting details here, including one of the AWS ES developers reaching out to ask how they can make their version compatible with Curator.  Another developer tried to hack a way to make it work, but found that there were no substitute calls for the metadata one, which also covers index state (open/closed) and routing information, both of which are considered essential for Curator.

The IndexList object in Curator 4 pulls the full list of indices, and all of their associated metadata at object instantiation time.  It escapes the loops and frequent API calls that were in Curator 3.x.  To suggest that I'm refusing to support the way Curator 3 worked is to miss the mark.  It was, and is, a rewrite for optimization and to support newer API calls, rather than a deliberate maneuver to hurt 3.x or AWS ES users. 

Please also remember that I wrote the documentation before AWS ES 2.x was released.  This has clearly resulted in some frustration for you, and I'm sorry for that.  I will alter the docs for all references to IAM credentials to point out that AWS ES is not supported, or perhaps remove them altogether from the documentation.

def get_elasticsearch():
    """ get hold of the Elasticsearch API via the settings """
    if not hasattr(settings, 'ELASTICSEARCH_HOSTS'):
        err = 'Missing ELASTICSEARCH_HOSTS'
        logger.error(err)
        raise Exception(err)

    kwargs = {
        'hosts': settings.ELASTICSEARCH_HOSTS,
        'use_ssl': settings.ELASTICSEARCH_USE_SSL,

    }
    if getattr(settings, 'ELASTICSEARCH_ENABLE_SNIFF', False) is True:
        sniff_conf = {
            'sniff_on_start': True,
            'sniff_on_connection_fail': True,
            'sniffer_timeout': 60,
            'retry_on_timeout': True
        }
        kwargs.update(sniff_conf)
    if hasattr(settings, 'ELASTICSEARCH_USER') and \
            hasattr(settings, 'ELASTICSEARCH_PASS'):
        kwargs['http_auth'] = (
            settings.ELASTICSEARCH_USER,
            settings.ELASTICSEARCH_PASS)
    if settings.ELASTICSEARCH_USE_SSL:
        kwargs['verify_certs'] = True
        kwargs['ca_certs'] = certifi.where()

    es = Elasticsearch(**kwargs)
    return es

We assume a string value for routing (or any other query parameters) so we just serialize them as strings. This means passing in `None` results in a query containing `routing=None` in the query parameters.

There are several possibilities how we can deal with this:

1. do nothing, document this behavior better
2. drop query parameters set to `None`
3. serialize `None` as empty string

I am not a fan of wither of these solutions, which one would people prefer?

This statement causes the wheel to end up with an unconditional requirement for futures if built with Python < 3:
```
+ (['futures'] if sys.version_info < (3, 0) else []),
```
Replacing that with:
```
, extras_require={
    ':python_version < "3.0"': ['futures']
},
```
will cause the condition to be properly reflected in the meta files inside the wheel that hold requirements.
Yes, I think you would need to pass `base_url` parameter for `ComputeManagementClient`. I think, the default is `https://management.azure.com` and for China cloud it could be different.

Also, our intent is that the version of the `azure` package should reflect the overall SDK version, as it will (should) pin the versions of the subpackages.

So installing `azure==x.y.z` may not get you the latest version of `azure-storage`, but it will always get the same one. Users who want/need a later patch to the storage APIs are able to directly update that, which is only reliable with `pip` (as used by the majority of our users) using the current meta-package approach as opposed to a single consolidated package.

We do appreciate that this makes it more difficult for redistribution and vendoring of the whole package, but that's a relatively rare scenario, and we get some significant workflow benefits from splitting up the packages.

(FWIW, `pip install --target <dir> azure` will get you all the extracted sources in an easily vendored/redistributable directory.)

pre-commit doesn't really do any attempt at fishing out installed executables.  It tries by default the executable you're using.

For `language: python_venv` when running pre-commit under python2, you'll need to tell pre-commit where to find a suitable executable via `language_version`:

```python
    -    id: liccheck
         language: python_venv
         language_version: python3
         ...
```

Note that for hooks which are being distributed, it is suggested to use `language: python` over `language: python_venv` as it is more portable.

defaulting `language: python3` would potentially lead to undesirable results if someone is expecting a newer / older `python3.x` version so I don't think (?) it's an appropriate default here

@asvetlov When we choose a timeout object for the session which contains uber timeout and connect timeout, and then start a request with a timeout object that cancels the uber timeout, usually we still want to keep the connect timeout. So timeout object should be able to merge.

I suggest that the timeout object has following interfaces:

0. timeout object is always immutable. We may consider implement it from NamedTuple.
1. `Timeout(uber_timeout = None, *, connect_timeout = None, ...)` constructor. Only the uber timeout can be specified as positional arguments, all other arguments are keyword-only. The default values are None. Accepted values are None / False / <positive numbers>. None means "use default", False means "force disable", other numbers are timeout values.
2. `copy(**kwargs)` Copy an existing timeout object and update the timeout values. Equivalent to convert the timeout to a dict, merge the dict with `kwargs` and use the dict to call `Timeout(**args)`.
3. `merge(timeout)` Copy an existing timeout object and merge the settings from another timeout object. Only False/<numbers> are merged, None values are ignored. Equivalent to `.copy(**timeout.todict())`
4. `todict()` Convert the timeout object to a dict containing settings that are not None. Can be used to implement __repr__ and for logging.

**NOTICE**: `copy(**kwargs)` is different from `merge(Timeout(**kwargs))`. `Timeout(connect_timeout=1).copy(connect_timeout=None)` returns a timeout object with connect_timeout=None, but `Timeout(connect_timeout=1).merge(Timeout(connect_timeout=None))` returns a timeout object with connect_timeout=1.

I'd like to add a vote for this issue, with one clarification: it should only scroll if the output is already scrolled down to the bottom.

I often run calculations the produce output over some extended time, maybe printing another line every ten seconds or so.  I want to sit and watch the output as it appears.  But once it collapses all the output into a scrollable text area, it stops being visible.  I can scroll the output down to the bottom, but when the next line appears, it's always hidden off the bottom.  So I have to sit there with the mouse hovering over it, ready to scroll it down by hand every ten seconds just so I can see the next line that's just appeared.  This gets really irritating!

Of course, I might also want to scroll back through the output and look over it while the code is still running.  If it kept jumping back to the bottom, that would also be irritating.  So this is the behavior I suggest.  If the cell's output is already scrolled to the bottom and new output appears, it should automatically scroll to make that new output visible.  If it's scrolled to anywhere other than the bottom, it should remain where it is when new output appears.

And to be clear, I'm talking about scrolling the output of a particular cell.  I'm not talking about scrolling the whole window.

I think the general idea in keyboard shortcut display is this: show the
"picture" (including case) that are on the users physical keyboard keys. To
type a lowercase "m" on a US based Mac/Windows keyboard, a user would
usually press the key that has the picure "M" on it. Our usage of uppercase
to display keyboard shortcuts matches best practices of OS styles for
shortcuts on Mac/Windows apps, which use uppercase. I agree it is a bit
confusing, but I think we are making the right choice and that changing it
would diverge from the current "best" (if you can call it that) practice.

The problem with that is that we would still hit https://github.com/OCA/pylint-odoo/issues/59#issuecomment-254951868.

@moylop260's solution implies that developers _should **not**_ manually increase `fix`, but rely in the automatic trigger instead.

But if the developer never increases `fix` and the trigger only increases `dev`, then `fix` would always be zero, and then `fix` would become useless, so we could simply remove `fix`... but then `dev` becomes `fix`, so it's the same as removing `dev` and leaving versioning schema exactly as it is right now.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import ast
import json
import os
import simplejson
import subprocess
import time
import concurrent.futures

from boto3.session import Session

def create_aws_session():
    """
    Create AWS Session
    """
    session = Session(
        aws_access_key_id=os.getenv("AWS_ACCESS_KEY"),
        aws_secret_access_key=os.getenv("AWS_SECRET_KEY"),
        region_name=os.getenv("AWS_REGION")
    )
    c = session.client('sqs')
    return c

def get_queue_url(c):
    """
    Get Queue URL
    """
    queue_name = "DeploymentEvents"
    response = c.get_queue_url(
            QueueName=queue_name
    )
    url = response['QueueUrl']
    return url

def check_sqs(c, url):
    """
    Pull SQS queue and look for new messages
    """
    messages = c.receive_message(
        QueueUrl=url,
        AttributeNames=['All'],
        MaxNumberOfMessages=1,
        VisibilityTimeout=120,
        WaitTimeSeconds=5
    )

    if messages.get('Messages'):
        message = messages.get('Messages')[0]
        body = message['Body']
        receipt = message['ReceiptHandle']
        status = deploy_service(body)
        if status:
            delete_msg(c, url, receipt)



def delete_msg(c, url, receipt_handle):
    """
    Delete message from SQS
    """
    response = c.delete_message(
        QueueUrl=url,
        ReceiptHandle=receipt_handle
    )

def deploy_service(message):
    """
    Deploy service to kubernetes
    """
    data = json.loads(message)
    image = data.get("image", None)
    namespace = data.get("namespace", None)
    deployment = data.get("deployment", None)
    if image is not None:
        if namespace is not None:
            if deployment is not None:
                return update_kube(image, namespace, deployment)
    return False


def update_kube(image, namespace, deployment):
    """
    Set image for deployment in namespace
    """
    # Force unicode to string
    image = str(image)
    namespace = str(namespace)
    deployment = str(deployment)
    print "starting deployment of {} on {}".format(deployment, namespace)

    # Because none of the py modules are mature enough
    # Using direct kubectl command
    switch_namespace_cmd = "kubectl config set-context aws_k8s --namespace={namespace}".format(namespace=namespace)
    set_image_cmd = "kubectl set image deployment/{deployment} {deployment}={image}".format(
        deployment=deployment,
        image=image
    )
    status = subprocess.call(switch_namespace_cmd, shell=True)
    if status == 0:
        status = subprocess.call(set_image_cmd, shell=True)
        if status == 0:
            return True
    return False


if __name__ == "__main__":
    client = create_aws_session()
    url = get_queue_url(client)

    while True:
        check_sqs(client, url)
        time.sleep(5)
```

Hey @JBKahn, @sheilatron et al.

Sorry about the lack of response — I've been super busy the past couple of weeks.

There are not *too* many things left to be done before 2.0 could be considered a release candidate. In any case, as you've noticed, the development version is in a working condition, even if some APIs might not be final.

---

Off the top of my head, perhaps the most important task is finalizing the new exception design and deciding what we want error messages to look like.

Historically, the messages are just strings, and even though you can provide your own format strings, the mechanism doesn't lend itself well to localization, for instance. Also, the formats have no standard structure — what `{0}` means is completely up to the type.

It's pretty clear that we should be constructing the messages in a more structured way that makes is easy to programmatically determine what went wrong. The new exceptions have laid the groundwork for this: the message objects are now a custom class instead of `str`, but I still haven't thought through how best to exploit that.

As always, it's not a hugely complex issue per se, but backward-compatibility considerations make it more so.

For example, I'm not sure the existing ability to provide your own message strings to the field instance is very useful. All I need is

* a basic human-readable message for **developers** to look at
* an ID such as `"max_value"` that indicates in a standardized way the constraint that was violated.

The program can then come up with the actual **user-facing** error message however it likes.

I'd love to hear how people are currently going about this in practice and what kind of solution would work best for them.

differentiate.

Re reverse-mode vs mixed-mode strategies, I realised a little while ago (to my disappointment) that, in an idealised implementation, there _isn't_ a difference in memory usage between hvps with mixed mode vs reverse mode.

In an idealised implementation, both use 2x the memory of calculating the gradient. I think the FLOPS would also be the same or very similar.

In our real, non idealised implementation mixed mode does seem to perform better in both memory and speed, by a modest factor (around 1.5x). I think this is for two reasons. Firstly in forward mode you don't have to record or traverse a graph, which reduces overhead. Secondly the jvps tend to have simpler forms than the vjps, for many primitives. Look at [einsum](https://github.com/BB-UCL/autograd-forward/blob/master/autograd_forward/numpy/numpy_grads.py#L222) for example, a primitive which I seem to use a lot.

> When a NAME_REGISTRATION transaction is committed, the NAME_PREORDER's address is debited the amount of STACKs the name cost.

Should the debit occur when the preorder is committed? Otherwise, an adversary could preorder a bunch of names, and only issue a register when they see another user's register hit the mempool without penalty (in STACKs). Granted, the limited validity window for preorders limits the scope of this, but I wonder if this is something we want to deal with.

@myint: I understand it's safer than previous versions, but isn't it useful to catch unused code?

It's sort of tough. I think in some ways it's analogous to the "unused imports" check – sometimes they're intentional because I'm re-exporting for a specific module, but usually they're just a mistake, and ultimately (with Flake8, which I understand is downstream of here) doing a `# noqa: F401` for those few unused imports that I explicitly want is pretty good.

It's weird. I understand what you're saying, and it makes sense, but this is the sort of check that e.g. both ESLint and RuboCop have in their recommended/default ruleset ([ESLint: no-unused-vars](https://eslint.org/docs/rules/no-unused-vars), [RuboCop: Lint/UnusedMethodArgument](http://rubocop.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cops_lint/#lintunusedmethodargument)).

In practice Pyflakes via Flake8 seems to be the de facto linting tool that everyone uses, so it's odd that Pyflakes doesn't flag something that most linters elsewhere flag, and that is usually an error, just because it's sometimes not so.

Desclaring an unused variable never sounds perfect to me. If it is done willingly one should reflect this using an appripriate name as _. 

pyflakes recently added an error for unused assigments in exceptions as in 

```python
    try:
       ...
    except (TypeError, KeyError) as e:
        return None 
```
This is the same situation, but it raises an error. It is just consequent to add my proposed feature.

Using locals() is kind of like `import *`, which pyflakes does warn about. I'd personally rather have pyflakes warn and give false positives for locals() meta-programming than give up and stop warning at all. At least then it might still catch unrelated bugs.

Checking if *write* is permitted is not [a straight-forward operation](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3696080/check-if-i-can-write-a-file-to-a-directory-or-not-with-python) (i.e. mounted as `ro` under unix, or permissions difficult to parse and locked files under windows) - the safest check is to actually try to *write and fail*.

I agree with @bibby & @magicrobotmonkey that
- `5xx` are for "extra-ordinary" server-problems, 
- `4xx` for client-side problems, which occasionally may require "ordinary" server-side interventions (i.e. enable a flag); the log message / http-response body should clarify those interventions, and this can be used to differentiate the responses.  

Now for the "comparison and rejection of identical packages", it is preferable to resolve it client-side, by comparing hashes, not to waste network-bandwidth.

More generally, if what you need is a "sync" operation, I would try to upload files with `rsync`, which is a tool made for this task.

I don't think there are any versions with dashes in them either.

Originally the version was required to be numeric (with components separated by `.`), and with the requirement to support Rawhide (#11) the other piece was added so that an arbitrary string works as well. I think tightening it up to at least prohibit dashes would not break anything, as Rawhide is the only release using non numeric version.

I think the regex works as intended, but that intention could be considered flawed: the version can be pretty much anything, but as long as it starts with a digit, it must follow the `X.Y.Z` format (for any number of components). This way looked saner that whitelisting `Rawhide` as the only exception.

I would like also to split example.models later to
- nomal examples that can be used in Readme examples (some also in simple tests)
- special and strange models for more complicated tests, e.g. backward compatibility tests

Some example models use lower_case field names, some use MixedCase and some use both. Lower case are fine with ORM, but problematic if also raw queries must be hand written. Custom fields are really a big problem how to write raw query correctly by hand and get the result from cursor.

A fix require to wait for some work and decisions:
- Split the query module to a DB API driver and Django backend query.
- Implement a normal cursor that returns primarily a list of values (what expects Django even for related fields), not only a tree of dictionaries as it is currently.

I think this is somewhat similar to #200. It is not easy to implement a reliable automated mechanism that adapts to the (potentially dynamic) environment.
In theory, system could auto-diagnose itself and understand whether the current bottleneck is opening connections, the streaming speed, the hdd speed etc. It could also transfer files using a smart strategy, where if the bottleneck is on the network, and the file/part is about to be finished, then another thread is already started, so that the connection could be established in time to take over the network from the previous file (saving a few seconds of network bandwidth). We have not planned on working towards such optimizations (nor even on simple thread count adaptive mechanism) in the nearby future.

If other cloud storage CLIs don't offer such functionality and we could offer it, that could become an easily benchmarkable competitive advantage of B2 as a service.

There is a valid use case for an option to disallow empty strings:

When you want to show a special error message for missing values including empty strings:

```python
name = fields.String(
    required="Please enter your user name.",
    validate=[
        validate.Length(min=4, max=20,
                           error="User name must have between {min} and {max} characters."),
        validate.Regexp(r"[a-zA-Z0-9_\-]*$",
                           error="User name must not contain special characters"
                                 "(except _ und -)")
    )
```

In that case, you cannot add a Length(min=1) validator like this:
```python
name = fields.String(
    required="Please enter your user name.",
    validate=[
        validate.Length(min=1, error="Please enter your user name."),
        validate.Length(min=4, max=20,
                           error="User name must have between {min} and {max} characters."),
        validate.Regexp(r"[a-zA-Z0-9_\-]*$",
                           error="User name must not contain special characters"
                                 "(except _ and -)")
    )
```
because an empty string would trigger two error messages (from both length validators):
```python
# you would get 2 'name'-errors:
# 'name': ['Please enter your user name.', 'User name must have between 4 and 20 characters.']
```

def fivepoints_to_frame(pnt):

    """

    Converts a 1 dimensional, dict-like singlediode or sapm result

    to a 5 row DataFrame with columns current and voltage.

    Users can iterate over the rows of a multidimensional

    singlediode or sapm result, if necessary.

    """

    ivframe = {'i_sc': (pnt['i_sc'], 0),

               'p_mp': (pnt['i_mp'], pnt['v_mp']),

               'i_x': (pnt['i_x'], 0.5*pnt['v_oc']),

               'i_xx': (pnt['i_xx'], 0.5*(pnt['v_oc']+pnt['v_mp'])),

               'v_oc': (0, pnt['v_oc'])}

    ivframe = pd.DataFrame(ivframe, index=['current', 'voltage']).T

    ivframe = ivframe.sort_values(by='voltage')



    return ivframe

fivepnts = pvsystem.singlediode(

Looks interesting. Some thoughts on PyRTM and the broader topic:

I would like to understand some of the pluses and minuses of the many models on https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atmospheric_radiative_transfer_codes for our application. Can anybody point me to some good literature? @Calama-Consulting's referenced paper says that SBDART is simple. @NelisW uses MODTRAN, which does not appear to be open source. RRTMG is now fairly standard in my field, though I don't personally have experience with it. SMARTS seems popular. 

On the one hand, we can just wrap a bunch of crap and call it a day. On the other hand, we have an opportunity to help lead the PV community, or at least set some good examples and make some good default choices for people like me that don't already know the literature.

Many of these tools are very general-purposed, and we should keep our focus on the application of these tools to PV. So, I think wrappers are generally the way to go, but I also don't want to introduce dependencies to projects that are no longer maintained. We also need to consider ease of installation. For example, PyRTM itself may be trivial to install, but what about its dependencies? Can we use conda to bundle the whole thing?

Might be wrong about this, but I think that since PyRTM is GNU-licensed you'd have to get permission from all contributors to pull any part of it into pvlib-python.

Hi @rueberger, hi @nimrand. 
Thanks a lot for your feedback on this. I'm very happy to see an issue that talks about larger design choices as opposed to a bug or small feature!   The issue of having "too much magic" is one that had me thinking for a long while, and so I'd like to provide a multi-part answer:

1. *Config Scopes*: The functions decorated with `@ex.config` are IMHO the place with the most magic in Sacred: Extracting locals without any obvious mechanism, blocking statements in response to updates, and introducing a `seed` variable. This is something that I've recently decided, that I'd like to change. I think it would be much better to have a `prepare_config` function that is injected with a special dict-like config object, and assigns values to that. The config object would support attribute access to its keys and be able to block assignments as needed. Example:
    ```python
    @ex.config
    def prepare_config(cfg):
        cfg.learning_rate = 0.1
        cfg.network.hidden_size = 100
        cfg.network.nr_layers = 4
    ```
    This would only be minimally more effort to write and much more obvious. 

2. *Experiments as objects*: I agree with @nimrand there, in that I prefer the instance + decorate workflow over inheritance. This helps to support a scripting workflow with minimal boilerplate, and while config injection is something you have to get used to, it is not *that* magical in my opinion.
That being said, I'd be happy to support both approaches, but I'm not sure yet how much work this would be. I'll have to think more about that. But this also brings me to my next point:

3. *Ingredients*: I'm very unhappy with ingredients. They are meant to provide a convenient means for modularizing an experiment. But thus far they have never worked to my satisfaction. They are too inflexible and break in unexpected ways. And this problem I relate to the concerns/confusion that @rueberger mentioned. So one thing that I would like to do is to completely overhaul how ingredients work. And so far the best way that I could think of was to have them be classes deriving from `Ingredient`, that would be instantiated inside the config. Something along these lines:
    ```python
    class Dataset(Ingredient):
        def __init__(self, cfg):
            super().__init__(cfg)
            self.path = '/home/user/datasets`
            self.normalize = False

    @ex.config
    def prepare_config(cfg):
        cfg.data = Dataset(cfg)
    ``` 
    This would solve many flexibility issues (e.g. using an ingredient multiple times, or using dynamically different ingredients based on some other config value). I haven't fully thought this through, but it seems doable with only mild magic. This would mean that ingredients have to be implemented as classes. Which I think is ok, given that you probably need to do some engineering anyways when you hit the point that you need ingredients. But it also bothers me to break the symmetry between experiment and ingredient. So that would be an argument for experiments as classes.... 

Ok. To sum up: I don't know a good solution to these issues yet. But I'd be very interested if these are the same "too much magic" issues that you had in mind, and to hear your thoughts on my suggestions.

@NikolajBjorner I didn't make it clear in my previous posts, and let me try to rephrase that.

I believe the gain in caching internalized formulae and lemmas is completely not comparable with the overhead of searching the result space. What I care about is retaining the searching result of the old solver in the newly-forked solver, so we don't have to search again (for all previously added clauses) when a new assertion is added to the new solver - instead, we only search a smaller space. I believe this is what incremental solving in Z3 is about. Let me know if I made a mistake here.

Yes ``shell`` and ``test-z3`` are less than ideal. I certainly could refactor them. The reason I haven't done so is because in principle you would want both those executables to link against the built ``libz3``. The CMake code would be considerably cleaner if this was done. Unfortunately this doesn't really seem possible right now due to

* The Z3 library hides most of its symbols so you get linking errors if you try to link ``shell`` or ``test-z3`` against the library
* I'm not sure if this was intentional but the existing build system creates separate ``install_tactic.cpp``, ``mem_initializer.cpp``, ``gparams_register_modules.cpp`` for the ``libz3``, ``shell`` and ``test-z3`` targets. This would potentially lead to linking problems because there would be multiple implementations of the same functions.

Because the existing build system has this problem I decided that fixing it right now was orthogonal to implementing a CMake build system that largely duplicates the behaviour of the python build system.

Sorry, I should have been more clear about the problem.  The distinction isn't between applying over columns versus applying over rows, which is what the `axis` argument does in `DataFrame.apply`.  We still want to call a function once for each row in a table.  The question is: does the function you're applying have this signature:

`def predictor(feature0, feature1, feature2, feature3):`

...or this one:

`def predictor(features_array):`

Currently `Table.apply` expects the first kind of function (with one argument for each column you apply the function to), and there's no direct way to use the second kind of function.  But when you're doing something like linear regression where the input is really just an array of anonymous numbers, the first kind of function would be very bad style, since you'd have to rewrite it every time you changed the number of features you were using.

I think since we have multiple uses for modals, we should probably outline the differences and some of the expected user experiences.

Our editor has two different kinds of modals, both of which get triggered by codemirror outside of the React flow and are rendered using their cursor stuff. Those two are:

* Introspection (the tooltips as in #2443)

* `about` menu (desktop gets to cheat for this one)'

# Constrain the first 28 bytes to be non-null and non-newline:

st.posix.files[0].length = 29

@mraspaud I started doing the `filter_parameters` changes (chose that instead of `filtering_parameters` and started to find some unnecessary code. First and maybe most importantly is that I removed the `sensor` keyword from the regular reader loading during `Scene.__init__`. This means that the `Scene` requires a list of filenames and the reader names used to read them. There is no check for `sensor` and there is no automatic reader finding. I also removed the `base_dir` keyword since it shouldn't be used anymore. This simplified the `ReaderFinder` class enough that I just turned it in to a function.

I started to consider moving all of the filename filtering stuff out of the reader base classes but then realized that it would require duplicating the config reading and stops subclasses from customizing any filename filtering.

Lastly, I'm wondering if we should remove the `start_time`, `end_time`, and `area` keywords as individual keywords to the readers and instead make them part of `filter_parameters` or other. They don't seem to be used (even by the custom `mipp_xrit` reader) for anything except filename or file handler filtering.

Although this is "aggressive" as far as removing features from the `Scene` init, I think it simplifies the init a ton so that new users are scared away by the number of options.

@olupton
ROOT is a slightly difficult to support Application for a few different reasons. Mostly this is to do with sourcing the environment correctly for the version of ROOT to be requested.

With this in mind. Can you either share what backend you're running or run a report(jobs(xx)) to share the full job configuration so that we can work out what's likely going wrong.

ROOT Dirac support for LHCb is a special case which has had some work done recently and I need to work on adding proper LHCb ROOT support to the other backends such as Batch, Local, etc.
We should be making use of ```SetupProject ROOT v604r2``` to get our LHCb ROOT environment but this is incompatible with the experiment neutral Core of Ganga which just wants ```$ROOTSYS``` to be defined.
We use the SetupProject method with LHCbDirac only so far.


Now for the details:

The ROOT application supports PyROOT and classic CINT(CLANG) scripts.
These are always executed in the fashion of:

```root.exe myScript.ext(someScriptArgs)```

Where the myScript.ext is either a python script or an uncompiled 'interactive' CINT(CLANG) script.
The difficulty is in passing the script arguments in a way which captures everything including user strings to the someScriptArgs part of the script.
i.e. If the user wants to send the arguments ```5``` and ```"foo"``` passed to the script ```myScript.C``` then we have to construct:
```myScript.C(5, "foo")```
(Note the use of ```"``` here I remember this being important at some stage)
The arguments here are assembled and put into a templated script later which means that they're a little fragile atm and are on my long todo list of applications to take a look at.

If you run a report on the job we will hopefully get the script that is trying to be executed it is possible your script either can't be run interactively as an argument to the ROOT executable or that some argument is being mangled in a way that ROOT no longer likes it at runtime

NB:
You can make use of ROOT commands from a short wrapper script to execute pre-compiled programs within the ROOT environment when submitting jobs and I've made a lot of use of this approach in the past.

Worked on this a bit more today. The behavior seems to be a bit different in pyramid 2.0 and pyramid 2.1. In pyramid 2.0, I see (from the logs) that the transaction is aborted, but then another transaction is created and that one commits. However, this isn't what fails the test. The test fails because the exception isn't turned into a failing 507 response. That bug gets fixed in 2.1 (I think it's https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid_tm/pull/61). However, now, the transaction doesn't get aborted; it doesn't commit as part of the response, but it does commit later (when it goes out of scope?), which causes subsequent requests to fail. (Actually, we got lucky; the DELETE to clean up is what fails, even though it probably shouldn't.)

Some of the error handling paths in pyramid_tm cause the transaction to be aborted. It might be a bug that prevents it from being aborted in this case. I'm going to file a bug upstream and see what they think.

Doesn't look like a match to me...  Frame 1 belongs to the same cluster as Frame 3 according to sklearn, but not according to cpptraj.  But you're also doing different analyses, aren't you?  @drroe can correct me if I'm wrong, but cpptraj will automatically cluster a trajectory based on RMSD, right?  This is effectively one feature per frame.  Whereas with sklearn you just asked it to cluster the trajectories based on the Cartesian coordinates, which is 3N features per frame (!!).

An equivalent interpretation is the sklearn approach is clustering on the *projection* of each trajectory on the Cartesian basis set (which is just the identity matrix) for each frame *for each "mode"*.  A much better coordinate system to use for this is the basis of principal components.  That way, you only need to use the projections of the first 2 to 5 modes on each frame, rather than all 3N modes, to get almost all the same information.  And the fact that you'll have at most 5 features in your clustering algorithm (rather than, say 5000) means better cluster definitions IMO.

If you wanted to really compare them, you need to give sklearn the RMSD time series rather than the trajectory.  Or give cpptraj 3N data sets -- the X, Y, and Z-coordinates of the trajectory for each atom.

Regarding 1), the gamma corresponds to the discount factor used in the RL algorithm. We want to normalize rewards so that the advantages in the RL algorithm have a nice magnitude, meaning that we should use the same gamma that's used to compute the advantages. The returns are being averaged over the entire course of training by `ret_rms`, so the gamma doesn't directly effect how fast our normalization coefficient is changing.

Regarding 2), subtracting the mean from the rewards would change the dynamics of the environment. For example, if your rewards are all 1, that is very different than if your rewards are all 0. In the former case, the agent wants to live; in the latter, it doesn't care if it lives or dies. The reward clipping looks like a bit of a hack, and there's probably no way to do it truly "correctly". Centering the clip range at the mean reward wouldn't quite make sense, I don't think, and I can't think of a perfect way to do it.

@chihuahua One more thought—I mentioned earlier that "The major advantage of exporting to gv over Ti*k*Z is that with Ti*k*Z we would have to lay out the graph ourselves." But in retrospect I realize that this was a silly comment—the graph explorer _already_ computes the layout of the graph; we have the layout right in the positioning! So I will revise my estimation:

  - If we export to gv, then we'll define the _structure_ of the graph and let dot(1) lay it out as it sees fit. This could be quite different from the layout shown in the dashboard.
  - If we export to Ti*k*Z, then we'll hard-code the current layout of the graph as computed by the dashboard. The relative positions of all nodes will be identical to the view in the dashboard (though their sizes may differ a bit). 

Each of these sounds valuable; I'm happy to try gv first, or not, as you prefer, but the implementations will likely be quite different (hooking into different places of the graph dashboard code).

I just checked and this is wrong. When `Blueprint.doc` is called on a view function, all documentation information is duplicated for every method this function answers to. I just modified the tests to make that clearer. And I fixed the example in the docstring.

The limitation is that when a function answers to multiple methods, the documentation is the same for all. I don't really see a practical case where one would have to use a function with multiple methods when designing a rest API. It generally ends up with multiple if/else cases in the function, and in the end I find it clearer to use different functions. Overall, this limitation does not bother me.

As a consequence, I think the docstring approach is fine after all, with no YAML needed.

There are currently no code examples showing how to do this, but it is certainly possible. All plotting functions in the Python client generate a JSON data structure describing the plot contents, and post the JSON to the server: https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/__init__.py#L246
The JSON format is defined by Plot.ly. Hence, you can generate the appropriate JSON in C++ or Java and send it to the server, which will visualize it.

Having said that, re-implementing and maintaining client logic in some other language is a lot of work. For the Lua client, therefore, we did the following: the Lua client sends a special JSON request to the server that contains the (Python client) function name and all the input arguments that one would pass to the Python client: https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/th/init.lua#L964
The server unpacks this special request and executes the corresponding Python client code to obtain the JSON that encodes the data Plot.ly needs: https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/server.py#L290
The advantage of this approach is that (1) it minimizes the amount of Lua code we have to write and (2) any changes in the Python client are automatically exposed in the Lua client.

If you'd like to add a C++ or Java client, I would propose to use a similar setup. It may not require any changes in the server side (although we should probably change the name of the `unpack_lua` function).

@albertored 
I agree that knowing the return code of a command is useful. However, I'm not ready to accept any solution to this problem until **cmd2** stabilizes and we have time to consider what solution is most universal. Currently **cmd2**'s parsing logic is being completely rewritten, tab completion is being extended with the addition of AutoCompleter, and its being broken up into multiple files.

The need for the feature you suggest does exist. Without it, using ``cmdqueue`` is fairly limited since commands keep running until then end in all cases. Therefore ``load`` can only be used in simple cases. I have typically used the more featured ``pyscript`` command when logic is needed to determine what to do after a command runs. But many times this involves more than a return code since the parsing of a command's output can also be useful in knowing what to do next. I've done this using a ``CmdResult`` class member. This class is defined in cmd2.py and I believe some of the example scripts show its usage. But that approach is probably overkill for a batch mode like you are using.

There are other ways to solve this that need to be examined. Perhaps the solution would include something syntactical like the ``&&`` operator in shells like **bash**. That provides more granularity than the ``exit_with_error_code`` flag which always results in exiting upon command failure. Sometimes a developer cares if a particular command fails and would want to exit, but other times the failure of that same command won't affect the next command in the list and exiting would be the wrong thing to do. Logging is usually enough in those cases.

I believe the best solution will be implemented if we have more time to consider all possibilities.

Thoughts?

I'm unhappy that Anymail is making you re-create the entire `personalizations` block in `esp_extra`, which means you have to repeat the message's `to` (and any other `personalizations` data that Anymail would normally populate from the message, including things like [SendGrid display-name bug workarounds](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/v4.0/anymail/backends/sendgrid.py#L180-L187)). But I'm glad you found a way to get it working until Anymail can be updated.

Really interesting idea -- thanks for the thought you've put into this. (Believe me, I understand the pain you're feeling with SendGrid recently.)

And it's clever to use the status APIs to determine if an ESP is usable. Because the ESP's *sending* APIs often stay up, accepting and queuing messages, even when the ESP is significantly delaying delivery. So you have to find some other way to determine if the ESP is "down" for sending purposes. (BTW, is that statuspage.io v2/summary.json endpoint documented anywhere?)

There would be a handful of problems to work through:

#### 1. Keeping track of the current preferred connection

Anymail deliberately doesn't maintain any persistent state, which avoids a whole lot of additional configuration overhead for users. I'm not sure how we could implement "save chosen backup as current preferred connection" within Anymail.


#### 2. Data can diverge when mixing ESPs

This is probably more of a user-education issue: sending to the same set of recipients through multiple ESPs leads to potentially-confusing data divided among those ESPs.

If you're using your ESP's unsubscribe management, for example, you'd want to avoid backup ESPs sending to recipients on the primary ESP's unsubscribe list. And you'd have to figure out how to handle unsubscribes coming from messages sent through a backup ESP. That sort of syncing is beyond the scope of Anymail. (Similar issues with ESPs' bounced/blocked recipient lists.)

You'd also have to be careful how you interpret open click and open rate data collected by one of the ESPs. (Or really, anything where ESPs maintain data on your behalf.)


#### 3. Deciding if an ESP is "up"

As I said earlier, I think using the status API is clever. But it also looks like it could be tricky to decide whether the ESP is "up" from the status summary.

The simplest approach would be, if there are *any* open incidents, consider it down. But a lot of incidents aren't about sending/delivery -- e.g., Mailgun recently had [a control panel outage](http://status.mailgun.com/incidents/vlms0wxbmj2f), and SparkPost had [delays in metrics and reporting](http://status.sparkpost.com/incidents/kmn9zy25j70g). I don't think I'd have wanted to switch to a backup ESP in either of these cases.

We could try to be smarter by looking for outages only in particular components. Each ESP labels its status components differently, so that would take some research. (E.g., SendGrid is currently showing "Mail Sending" as "Degraded Performance," but all three of its subcomponents as "Operational" -- including the ones that track "the flow of mail generated by mail.send API requests.") It could also be fragile to future status page changes.

Another problem is that (some) ESPs [may not promptly list](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=12144071) (some) service issues in their status pages. (Though I suppose you're no worse off in that case than you are now.)


**All that said, though,** I threw together a quick and **untested** implementation of a [backend that checks status APIs](https://gist.github.com/medmunds/e6b837bb3b382098d775cb412b889632) and sends through the first "up" ESP. To avoid the whole persistent-state problem, it checks the status API(s) on *every* send. (Optimization is "left as an exercise for the reader." As is figuring out my bugs in it. :smile:) Feel free to try it out, fork and edit, and if you end up with something useful we can either add it to Anymail or at least to the docs as an advanced example.

Hey, I ran into the exact same problem. My conclusion was that there is something strange with how python loads variables.
Basically CPython compiles and runs your code as a module (because apparently that's the only way python can execute multiple statements). When compiling code as a module Python expects (from my observations) that on the module level `globals() is locals()`. As a result the lookup of `global_dict` in your case is done in the `py::globals()` which does not contain `global_dict` because when you set it, it gets added to the locals dictionary.

The workaround I found is to use the same dictionary for globals and locals in py::exec. You have to 'copy' stuff defined on a global level manually and don't forget to copy `__builtins__` because otherwise python will be confused.

Hi @faerietree,

I was asked to take a look at this issue by @SwartzCr to try and answer questions about how Let's Encrypt (& Boulder, the server-side CA part of Let's Encrypt that Certbot talks to) handles 301 redirects. It looks like I'm a little late since you already have things working (Woo hoo! :tada: :lock:) but I wanted to try and add some more information anyway.

> Failed authorization procedure. hamag-maschinenbau.de (tls-sni-01): 

We can rule out anything strange happening because of 3xx redirects in this case because you're using the TLS-SNI-01 challenge type. Redirects are an HTTP level concern that only apply to the HTTP-01 challenge type.

> This may indicate that a 301 redirect is cached (actually in most recent firefox clearing cache not helps) but how is this handled by certbot?

As mentioned, we know TLS-SNI-01 doesn't know about redirects but even in the case of HTTP-01 challenges any 301 redirects are **never** cached by Let's Encrypt's validation authority (VA). When it tries a HTTP-01 challenge it always does it without any past knowledge.

>  But how can the server<->client comm fail. There are no firewalls.

When I look at the VA logs from our side it seems like there was nothing listening on :443 or that something prevented the connection. Could your ISP/Host have some additional firewall or connection filtering in place?

Unfortunately I likely can't help understand why TLS-SNI-01 connections to the Apache plugin's challenge handler on :443 were being rejected. Hopefully @SwartzCr or another Certbot developer can help with that. I notice the `:443` handler is in a ` <IfModule mod_ssl.c>` block - is it possible that module isn't installed/loaded? (I'm probably stretching past my own ability to debug & pulling at strings :-))

I hope having a working webroot based issuance process and understanding the HTTP redirects a little better is helpful! Take care,
