Leveraging what will be in the certbot-auto, you could simplify it even more.

```Dockerfile
FROM alpine:3.5

RUN apk --no-cache add \
        bash \
        openssl \
        wget

# certbot
RUN cd /usr/bin \
    && wget https://dl.eff.org/certbot-auto \
    && chmod a+x certbot-auto \
    && certbot-auto --version --non-interactive
...
```

> 1) --webroot-path or -w options are being ignored.

Flags that begin with `--webroot` apply to the [webroot plugin](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#webroot) not the [manual plugin](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#manual).

> 2) with --staging option it created the SSL stuff in .../live/ folder however the full chain contained something with Fake CA X1

Yes. The `--staging` obtains certificates from Let's Encrypt staging/test environment which does not issue trusted certificates. This is explained in the help output for the flag:

```
  --test-cert, --staging
                        Use the staging server to obtain test (invalid) certs;
                        equivalent to --server https://acme-
                        staging.api.letsencrypt.org/directory (default: False)
```

> 3) Can you please make it super non-interactive.
>
> --agree-send-ip
> --agree-renew

The `manual` plugin will never be completely non-interactive as it's name suggests. It exists for users that we currently can't completely automate the process for in some way. This could be because they can't run Certbot on their server directly, they have load balancers sending traffic to multiple servers, etc. Based on the command you were using and your questions, it sounds like you want the webroot plugin. I linked to our documentation on the webroot plugin above. You can also find out more information about webroot by running `certbot --help webroot`.

If you do want to use the manual plugin, you can learn more about it by running `certbot --help manual` or by visiting the manual link above. There you will find this flag:

```
  --manual-public-ip-logging-ok
                        Automatically allows public IP logging. (default:
                        False)
```

which is the `--agree-send-ip` flag you asked for. Additionally, you probably want to be using the [renew](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewing-certificates) subcommand rather than `certonly`. This command will essential be a noop when your certificates are more than 30 days away from expiration and will renew your certificates otherwise.

If you do want to use the `certonly` subcommand for some reason, you can use the following flag as shown in our help output:
```
  --force-renewal, --renew-by-default
                        If a certificate already exists for the requested
                        domains, renew it now, regardless of whether it is
                        near expiry. (Often --keep-until-expiring is more
                        appropriate). Also implies --expand. (default: False)
```

It seems like the main problem here is you struggled to find the necessary instructions for your use case. Can I ask where you learned about Certbot and what documentation you were using? If there's any way we can smooth out this process for other people in your situation, we'd like to do so.

Let me know if you have any more questions.

I looked up RFC2616 and i think patf mixed something up here. The sentence "Since the redirection might be altered on occasion, the client SHOULD continue to use the Request-URI for future requests." is true for 302 Redirects, not for 301. 
I did some testing and most Browsers seem to cache 301 for an unlimited time if you dont use Cache-Control headers. So the result is "lets encrypt and never go back".
At least the "safe" option in the wizard should clearly communicate to the user that this redirect is permanently and cacheable on client side. Better would be something like three option: "easy" (no redirect), "safe temporary" (with 302 for testing) and "safe permanently" (with 301).

- Separate vhost blocks get merged, ending up with configuration that mots likely points vhost to a wrong documentroot, log files etc.

Please use "--renew-hook" as a synonym to "--deploy-hook" and do **not** handle them differently. Please.

I also saw this issue in the past, it's not related to Raiden node itself, but Parity sometimes losing historical db and needing a full resync to recover itself (and then Raiden not fetching the events it needs to populate the tokens list and everything else).

To check it's a Parity issue, do the following:
1. Open a browser with Metamask on Ropsten network
2. Create a filter in the default token network registry, for the TokenNetwork event:
```
filter = web3.eth.filter({fromBlock: 0, toBlock: 'latest', address: '0x445d79052522ec94078dfccf96d9302775ca5b4e', topics: ['0xf11a7558a113d9627989c5edf26cbd19143b7375248e621c8e30ac9e0847dc3f']})
```
3. Fetch the logs: `filter.watch(console.log)`
4. Repeat from 1., but now pointing Metamask to the `localhost:8545` or your local parity node

Here, on first case, the `filter.watch` outputed the 6 currently registered token events, while on second case, nothing. After a full parity resync, the second case also got me the tokens.

I dug a bit deeper into the django RelatedPopulator code and discovered that the related instances are in fact being instantiated correctly, i.e. the child models are being created and set in field caches

```python
# Fetch the instance with appropriate select_related.
instance = PlainModel.objects.select_related('relation__childmodel').first()
# You can access the ChildModel by the following, this will NOT incurr additional queries
instance.relation._state.fields_cache['childmodel']
```

The reason why the trying to fetch the childmodel instance via `instance.relation.childmodel` causes additional queries can be seen here https://github.com/django-polymorphic/django-polymorphic/blob/master/polymorphic/models.py#L174

I've taken @WhyNotHugo 's code snippet above and made the following change that retains the optimizations of `select_related` whilst also instantiating the instances properly.
```
def polymorphic_iterator(*fields):
    class PolyMorphicModelIterable:
        def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
            self.iterable = ModelIterable(*args, **kwargs)

        def __iter__(self):
            for obj in self.iterable:
                for field in fields:
                    instance = getattr(obj, field)
                    real_instance_name = instance.polymorphic_ctype.model
                    real_instance = instance._state.fields_cache[real_instance_name]
                    setattr(obj, field, real_instance)
                yield obj

    return PolyMorphicModelIterable


class PolymorphicRelatedQuerySet(models.QuerySet):
   """A class with a relationship to a polymorphic model should use this queryset"""
    def select_polymorphic_related(self, *fields):
        """
        Specify fields that should be cast to the real polymorphic class.

        Note that you still need to pass each field and the proper subclasses
        as `select_related`. You might want an auxiliary method in your
        queryset to do this if it's a common scenario.
        """
        obj = self.select_related(*fields)
        obj._iterable_class = polymorphic_iterator(*fields)
        return obj
```

Look at the [`shared` option of tasks](https://vatlab.github.io/sos-docs/doc/documentation/Remote_Execution.html#Option-shared-1312), we allow tasks to return variables. The variables exist as individual variables, and we somehow make them a dictionary from multiple substeps. 

So logically speaking, tasks belong to substeps, and the variables shared by tasks will appear as themselves in the namespace after `task` (although no statement is allowed there). For the same reason, variables from substeps (without task) should be shared in the same fashion to be consistent. 

That is to say, if we are to implement `shared` in a consistent way, we will have to follow the model here and return `a = {0: 1}` from the following step:

```
[10: shared='a']
a=1
```
except for `step_input` and `step_output` which are designed to be step level variables. 

If we really want to return `a=1` for the above example, we will have to maintain two sets of variables. A possible implementation is that:

1. We define a step namespace with variables such as `step_output` etc. It will also contain substep variables, which should be the variable for the last substep.
2. We define a special variables that consists of namespaces for each substep, something like `_vars`. At the end of step, this `_vars` will contain namespaces from substeps, such as `_vars={'0': {'a': 1}}`. 
3. It is up to the user how to share variables. In most cases users would simply share step level variables and

```
[10: shared='seed']
input: for_each={'i': range(10)}
import random
seed=random.randint(0, 10000)
```
will only return one of the `seed`. If he wants all the seeds, he can use

```
[10: shared={'seed': 'x["seed"] for x in _vars.values()'}]
input: for_each={'i': range(10)}
import random
seed=random.randint(0, 10000)
```
to get them from the `_vars` namespace. Of course `_vars` could provide some convenient function such as `_vars.get('seed')`.
SoS executes steps in separate processes so there has to be some way to pass `data` from master to workers, and `pickle` is not necessarily faster than `yaml.load`.

@damazter I think I understand what you mean. I made a bunch of changes to how the coordinates are handled internally. Now all the setpoint coordinates are stored internally (`self._x` etc).

Before, when setting a coordinate, the current values were measured and then adjusted. This would result in an incremental error during a loop when for example the magnet does not go fully towards its setpoint.

Now, the coordinates stored internally are independent from the 'actual' magnet fields, and for every `set` they are all updated.

The code could be cleaner however, I could not think of an easy way to have all the coordinate parameters interact such that they all update when one is changed.

My new proc_df does result in mean 0 standard deviation of 1, which is what cells [9] and [10] check. If you want I can run a df.describe() on them too and upload a new gist. Original proc_df is in cells [6] and [7] which shows that even though column num_2 is drawn from standard normal, the std_deviation is too low (.2295) because we've filled so much of the column with the mean/median before scaling. I would argue that changes our representation of the distribution in a way that we don't want by making it much more probability concentrated around the mean/median and with less tail probability.

I created every column from standard normal data, and in cell [12] we can see that old proc_df scaled values to be outlandish (-9.46 standard deviations away from mean 0 from a standard normal distribution at index 999998 on the validation set using old proc_df, compared to -2.17 standard deviations away from mean 0 with new proc_df). You would expect standardizing distributions that are already standard normal would hardly change their values. Old proc_df changes them greatly, new proc_df barely changes them. I can add a df.head() of the original train/valid so you can see original and scaled values if you want.

So with old proc_df, if we imagine the distribution, we scaled to a normal distribution that was much taller and skinnier than standard normal, and the StandardScaler transformed the data into a normal distribution that is much fatter and wider than standard normal. This is all knowing that the true data distribution was drawn from standard normal. New proc_df correctly fits a standard normal distribution, and transforms the data into a standard normal distribution.

So, stdout/stderr are currently redirected to files under `/tmp/raylogs/` (one stdout file and one stderr per process). The files will appear on the same machine that the process is on.

To view the logfiles through the Ray API, you can run `ray.global_state.log_files()`.

Note that stdout/stderr are not always flushed immediately, but this can be addressed by adding calls like

```python
import sys
sys.stdout.flush()
sys.stderr.flush()
```

@pcmoritz @robertnishihara I have removed `log4cplus` package. 
1. Though `glog` has no DEBUG level, we still can do it by ourselves. 
    - If a log is using DEBUG level but the threshold is higher than DEBUG, there will be no log.
    - If a log is using DEBUG level and the threshold is also DEBUG. There will be log with `glog` level INFO.
2. If `google::InitGoogleLogging` is not called, there will be no back trace when check fails or fatal log is used. To improve the debug log of main path, I add `RayLog::StartRayLog` to raylet main function.
3. If `RayLog::StartRayLog` has an empty `log_dir`, the log won't output to file (only in stderr).

I showed you a workaround for this:

1. Put the customization in its own variable: `cust = activity.customization()`
1. take the n'th widget in the widget list `w = cust.widgets()[n]`
2. update the widget in place `w.update({})`
3. resave the full list of widgets using `cust._save_customization()`

Due to the way python works (reference to the widget remains part of the list from which you took the widget), the widget is updated in place.

> Nominally async/await is still provisional

Not anymore :)

> can we do anything about how easy it is to forget an await? In retrospect async functions shouldn't implement __call__ IMO...

We discussed this at length on python-dev. Here's a summary: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0492/#pep-3152. The most important point is this one:

```python
# The following code:

  await fut
  await function_returning_future()
  await asyncio.gather(coro1(arg1, arg2), coro2(arg1, arg2))

# would look like:

  cocall fut()  # or cocall costart(fut)
  cocall (function_returning_future())()
  cocall asyncio.gather(costart(coro1, arg1, arg2),
                        costart(coro2, arg1, arg2))

```

I'd say changing core async/await semantics at this point is not an option. However, we can make it an opt-it (probably). 

## Idea

One way to implement this is to add a new attribute to frame objects `f_inawait` and a new opcode `BEFORE_AWAIT` to set it. So coroutine functions like the one below

```python
async def foo():
    print('hello')
    await bar()
```

would produce the following opcode:

```
  2           0 LOAD_GLOBAL              0 (print)
              2 LOAD_CONST               1 ('hello')
              4 CALL_FUNCTION            1
              6 POP_TOP

  3           8 BEFORE_AWAIT
             10 LOAD_GLOBAL              1 (bar)
             12 CALL_FUNCTION            0
             14 GET_AWAITABLE
             16 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             18 YIELD_FROM
             20 POP_TOP
             22 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             24 RETURN_VALUE
```

Where `BEFORE_AWAIT` would set `f_inawait` to `1` for the current frame, and `GET_AWAITABLE` would reset it back to `0`.

Now, we have [`sys.set_corotuine_wrapper()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.set_coroutine_wrapper) that allows to intercept creation of coroutines. We can use it to raise an error if a coroutine was constructed outside of an await expression:

```python
def coro_wrapper(coro):
    if not sys._getframe(1).f_inawait:
        raise RuntimeError(
            'creating a coroutine outside of await expression')
    return coro

sys.set_coroutine_wrapper(coro_wrapper)
```

I made a PoC implementation here: https://github.com/1st1/cpython/tree/coro_nocall. And here's a script illustrating how it works: https://gist.github.com/1st1/1cc67287654cc575ea41e8e623ea8c71

If you and @dabeaz think that this would significantly improve trio/curio usability, I can draft a PEP. /cc @ncoghlan

> I need to find a way to encapsulate the `RpcClient.receiver` task; right now the user of the class has to spawn it themselves which feels odd.

Yeah, this is annoying! My highest priority for 0.3.0 is implementing "simplified nurseries", where it will stop being mandatory for the parent task to park itself in `__aexit__` (more details: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/136#issuecomment-317972358). One of the reasons I'm excited about this is that it helps in this kind of case: you can make it so your users write

```python
async with open_rpc_client(...) as client:
    ...
```

and then `open_rpc_client` is something like:

```python
@asynccontextmanager
async def open_rpc_client(...):
    client = await _make_connection(...)
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.start_soon(client.receiver)
        try:
            yield client
        finally:
            # shut down the receiver
            nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
```

> Hi Zeb,

> On your points: Yes, yes and yes. I'll deal with the F2x business here mostly. The other stuff I'll promise I'll implement in the future!
> 
> The treatment of F2x is inconsistent at the moment. F2x and tcrecs (both specifiable by the user) are used to generate the q coefficients that govern the temperature change at each forcing time step. The forcing routine calculates the CO2 forcing which then feeds back into the temperature calculation (through F).
> 
> It sounds counter-intuitive, but with the way the model interacts with F2x and ECS, then the lower the value of F2x the greater the temperature change if net non-CO2 forcing is positive. If you have a low value of F2x then it takes less forcing to generate the same temperature change, so anything on top of this will be more effective than under a high F2x.
> 
> If we allow the user to specify F2x, then either this makes the forcing scale factor for CO2 redundant (the first element in the scale option; probably a desirable outcome) or there is an inconsistency (in that the user-specified value may not agree with the number coming out of the forcing routine). CO2 forcing uncertainty comes from the carbon cycle, so I don't think we need to implement a further scaling factor on top of this. In that case I think I should adjust the forcing routine to ensure that it matches the user-specified F2x. This was done in v1.0 with the Myhre forcing and in v1.1 with Etminan (but incorrectly).

I have the following evidence that this is a bug in nrfutil.  The image below comes from an instrumented version of the connectivity application.
![issue-147-diag](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/964298/40578039-c332e8b8-60d2-11e8-8717-ad1e8cb5b4ce.png)

For orientation:
* **19 TxAlloc** signal goes high when `m_tx_buffer` in `ser_hal_transport` is allocated and goes low when it's freed.
* **20 EvtTx** signal goes high when the TX buffer is allocated for `ser_conn_ble_event_encoder`, and low when it's freed.
* **21 CmdTx** signal goes high when the TX buffer is allocated for `ser_conn_command_process`, and low when it's freed.
* **22 RxActive** signal goes high when `phy_events_handler` in `ser_hal_transport` sets `m_rx_state` to `HAL_TRANSP_RX_STATE_RECEIVING`, and low when `m_rx_state` is set to `HAL_TRANSP_RX_STATE_RECEIVED`.
* **2 RxProcess** goes high when `ser_conn_rx_process` calls `ser_conn_received_pkt_process` and low when the call returns.
* **3 TxActive** signal goes high when `ser_hal_transport_tx_pkt_send` in `ser_hal_transport` sets `m_tx_state` to `HAL_TRANSP_TX_STATE_TRANSMITTING`, and low when `phy_events_handler` sets it to `HAL_TRANSP_TX_STATE_TRANSMITTED`.

In most of the image event transmission and command transmission interleaves nicely.  Although a command reception (22) can overlap with an event transmission (20), the transmission completes before the command is processed (2).

At the right side this breaks:

* At 8.640 ms **20 EvtTx** goes high, indicating the transmit buffer is reserved for an event.  The HAL transport gets the `SER_PHY_HCI_SLIP_EVT_PKT_SENT` event and `hci_tx_fsm_event_process` enters `WAIT_FOR_ACK`.
* At 8.643 ms **22 RxActive** goes high, and then **2 RxProcess** gets to `ser_conn_command_process` where it spins waiting for the transmit buffer that's being used by the event.

For the transmit buffer to be released, a `SLIP/PKT_RECEIVED` event has to be processed.  This never happens: other instrumentation shows that `ser_phy_uart_evt_callback` processes no additional data after the command is received, so the fault seems to be on the host side.

My hypothesis is nrfutil is waiting in the loop for the response to the command that it sent, and there's no thread available to processes the pending SLIP queue.

If [Nordic case 207383](https://devzone.nordicsemi.com/f/nordic-q-a/34791/ble-connectivity-state-machine-error) is fixed the transmit ultimately fails and `SER_PHY_EVT_HW_ERROR` ultimately makes it back to the connectivity application, but by this point nrfutil has aborted with the traceback above.

If my analysis is correct, this is a bug in the host-side Python/Boost infrastructure, in that processing SLIP messages does not account for interleaved transactions.  I'm not sure why nobody else sees it or why it shows up so often in my environment; maybe it's due to recent changes and most folks aren't using the current code with nRF51 devices.  In any case, the best I can do without replacing the host-side implementation is detect that it's happened and restart the update process until the complete image gets transferred.

If my analysis is not correct I'd appreciate any clues that might help me find a better workaround.

Actually it's quite important MEDIA_ROOT to be different from STATIC_ROOT and MEDIA_URL from STATIC_URL.


From Django Documentation:

>In previous versions of Django, it was common to place static assets in MEDIA_ROOT along with user-uploaded files, and serve them both at MEDIA_URL. Part of the purpose of introducing the staticfiles app is to make it easier to keep static files separate from user-uploaded files.

>For this reason, you need to make your MEDIA_ROOT and MEDIA_URL different from your STATIC_ROOT and STATIC_URL. You will need to arrange for serving of files in MEDIA_ROOT yourself; staticfiles does not deal with user-uploaded files at all. You can, however, use django.views.static.serve() view for serving MEDIA_ROOT in development; see Serving other directories.
